title: feat: Add batch assert version to person updates

labels.name: waiting
labels.description: Prevents stale-bot from marking the PR as stale.

body:
## Problem

The current person updates are not assert versions. Without version assertions, concurrent updates could silently overwrite each other, leading to lost updates in high-contention scenarios, this is mainly for race conditions during merges .

## Changes

Adds `ASSERT_VERSION` mode to batch person writing with retry logic:

- New `updatePersonsBatchAssertVersion()` SQL method that uses UNNEST with version assertion to detect conflicts
- Batch retry with refresh, when we get a version mismatch, it re-fetches only failed persons and retries as a batch 
- Handles person merges during retry by updating pending updates with new person IDs/UUIDs
- Fallback to individual updates only after max retries exhausted or on properties size violations (there's no easy way around the later)

Of note: Successfully updated persons are never retried. Only persons that failed due to version mismatch are included in subsequent retry attempts.

Also has a bit of a refactor:
- Extracted `batch-update-utils.ts` with shared batch preparation and result processing logic
- Extracted helper methods in `BatchWritingPersonsStore` for clearer separation of concerns

title: fix(ci): Fix flaky Playwright tests blocking test reruns
body:
## Summary

- Fixed flaky `annotations.spec.ts` test by adding explicit waits and better error handling
- Skipped `insight-variables.spec.ts` test that requires missing demo data setup

## Changes

### annotations.spec.ts
- Added explicit wait for modal to open before filling input
- Added timeouts (10s for button visibility, 5s for input, 10s for table update)
- Used locator variables instead of direct selectors for better readability
- Prevents race conditions that cause intermittent failures

### insight-variables.spec.ts
- Skipped test with detailed FIXME comment
- Test requires `InsightVariablesDataGenerator` which isn't run by default in demo data
- Without the "Insight variables" dashboard, test will always fail
- Added explanation of how to properly fix this (either add to default demo data or create custom setup)

## Why

These two tests were consistently failing on reruns, blocking the CI pipeline. The annotations test had timing issues, while the insight-variables test was missing required test data.


---

> [!NOTE]
> This PR was created by [Mendral](https://mendral.com). I'll respond to code review feedback automatically.

title: fix(data-viz): use immutable updates in Y-axis settings reducers
body:
## Problem

The SQL Insights visualization has a state mutation bug. 

When adding multiple Y-axes, editing the settings of the second axis intermittently copies those settings to the previous axis.

This happens because `lodash.merge` [mutates the target object in place](https://lodash.com/docs/#merge). 

Since the `updateSeries` reducer was performing a shallow copy of the state array, the underlying `settings` object reference was shared between series items.

Closes #30932

## Changes

Replaced mutable lodash.merge usage with immutable spread syntax (...) in dataVisualizationLogic.ts.

Specifically updated updateSeries and updateSeriesIndex to create fresh state objects on every update.

Removed the lodash.merge import.

## How did you test this code?

I added a unit test in `dataVisualizationLogic.test.ts` to reproduce the bug. 

It fails against `master` (confirming the mutation) and passes with this fix.

Verified locally that the test fails when the fix is reverted and passes when applied.

## Publish to changelog?

No.

title: feat(signals): Enrich transcription with metadata
body:
## Problem

- Can't track the Golden path at scale with free text, need chains of events.
- Summaries (and segments/points within) aren't searchable without embeddings.
- Including autocapture events in video-first summaries prompts heavily bloated the cost, not scalable.

## Changes

- As I significantly reduced the problems with unreliable clicks/timestamps, we can rely way more on DB data, instead of LLM.
- I updated LLM video-first prompts to return labels of interactable elements/blocks/pages together with the transcription.
- I heavily filtered autocapture events and am now able to attach them properly to interactions within video segments, without the need to feed DB data into the LLM.

Now, in addition to the regular transcription (left, which can be used for further summaries), we are also getting autocapture-like events to store in DB, to mark clicks/hovers/navigation/loading/input/etc. (right). And all of it is crazy cheap and scalable.

Events are structured and can be linked to ts/URLs/event ids/etc. When I'm back, I'll start storing them and use them for non-embedding search and as a timeseries to calculate Golden paths and deviations.

**Bonus:** Because event chains are built explicitly on the labels/page titles/etc. of the actual website, we also get a reliable taxonomy for feature detection.

title: ix(flags): Ensure unencrypted remote config flags are not hidden by postgres fallback
body:
**Summary**
This PR fixes a regression introduced in PostHog/posthog#46758 where unencrypted remote config flags were being filtered out by the PostgreSQL fallback cache.

**The Bug**
The query `~Q(is_remote_configuration=True, has_encrypted_payloads=True)` incorrectly filtered out rows where `has_encrypted_payloads` was `NULL` (which is the default for many older flags) due to SQL ternary logic (`NOT (True AND NULL)` evaluates to `NULL`).

**The Fix**
Updated the query to explicitly include flags that are either:
1. Not remote configuration.
2. Remote configuration but unencrypted (explicitly checking for `False` or `NULL`).

**Fixes PostHog/posthog-js#3061**

title: fix: encode trace and session IDs in URLs to handle special characters
body:
## Problem
Users were encountering 404 errors when accessing LLM traces that contained special characters (like `/` or `:`) in their IDs. The router was interpreting these characters as URL path separators instead of part of the ID string.

## Fix
Updated `frontend/src/products.tsx` to wrap `id` parameters in `encodeURIComponent()` for both:
- `llmAnalyticsTrace`
- `llmAnalyticsSession`

This ensures that generated URLs are safe (e.g., converting `/` to `%2F`), allowing the router to correctly capture the full ID.

## Related Issue
Fixes #47019

title: fix(ai): instruct model to parenthesize BETWEEN clauses to avoid parser bug
body:
Summary
Updates the system prompt in posthog/hogql/ai.py to explicitly instruct the AI to wrap BETWEEN operations in parentheses. Also removes a stale TODO comment from the grammar definition string.

Motivation
A TODO in the grammar definition identified a known parser precedence issue: // TODO(ilezhankin): BETWEEN a AND b AND c is parsed in a wrong way

The parser incorrectly groups the second AND into the BETWEEN clause. Instead of risking a complex compiler rewrite, we can reliably mitigate this by prompting the AI to output defensive SQL: (x BETWEEN a AND b) AND c. This prevents the ambiguity from ever reaching the parser.

Changes
Removed the TODO comment (saving tokens).

Added a specific rule to IDENTITY_MESSAGE requiring parentheses for BETWEEN clauses.

title: feat(propdefs): prototype property definitions filter builder service
body:
⚠️  **WIP do not merge yet!** ⚠️ 

## Problem
Property definitions dataset is extremely duplicative. It is drawn from the event stream, where once a new event type and/or custom properties are submitted, they are resubmitted regularly.

The service only needs to observe and persist them once, barring occasional manual edits performed by users and admins. Writing the same data optimistically to Postgres at this level of duplication is causing performance degradation on the database for no reason.

## Changes
This service will scan the known property definitions of selected teams, and build a set of serializable Bloom filters for each team. It will persist a row in the new `posthog_propertyfilter` table for each team, which can be fetched and cached by the `property-defs-rs` service.

If the prototype is successful (serialized filters are of reasonable size, caching is effective, and false positive rate is suitable low) these filters can be used to cheaply deduplicate properties found in the event stream, and minimize database writes from `property-defs-rs`.

In production, once a full static (all teams) filter set has been populated, a crawler process adapted from this prototype will track teams that have written previously-unseen property and event definitions recently, and update the corresponding Bloom filters offline in a cronjob.

## Does this work well for both Cloud and self-hosted?
Yes

## How did you test this code?
Locally, CI, and test deploy in `dev` env

title: feat(propdefs): override team_id & project_id if inbound raw Event includes root_team_id
body:
## Problem
New environments handling has replaced `project_id` with a notion of parent ("root") team ID which controls the global property def type classifications and other settings for all child teams.

We need the `property-defs-rs` service to become `root_team_id` aware as part of the transition.
⚠️ **DO NOT MERGE** until environments prep work is completed ⚠️ 


## Changes
* Write path: ensure incoming `Event`s override `team_id` and `project_id` when decomposed into `Update`s whenever `Event#root_team_id` is present
* Read path: rely on `team_id` in the property defs API where project_id was needed

_TODO_: remove uses of `COALESCE` on `project_id`, falling back to `team_id`, relying on (overridden) `team_id` in all `property-defs-rs` SQL

## Does this work well for both Cloud and self-hosted?
Yes

title: feat(hogql): type all CH functions
body: Feels like previous swings at this got us closer but never quite got fully over the line because: - Defining signatures for some functions was arduous, lots of careful hand-writing nested tuples etc - hard to just say "this thing takes any two numbers of the same type and returns a number of that type" - Some CH functions are pretty gnarly, doing things like `fn arrayMap B>(f: F, d: Vec) -> Vec` (in rust terms). That sigs got 3 generic arguments, and one of them's parameterised over the other two! I'm fairly-sure it would be impossible to write that type signature in the current system, even ignoring the fact there's no `LambdaType(ConstantType)` - or it would at least be be extremely awkward.. - There is always bigger fish to fry, and point 1) makes spending days handwriting and debugging type signatures a non-flier. This meant we never got all signatures types, so could never assert all signatures had a type, which means new signatures added mostly don't have types, and so on. Chicken, egg, etc With that in mind, the plan here is: - Introduce generics to the hogql function declarations, taking a "push down" approach where you declare up-front the set of types (or other generics) the placeholder expands to, and then use it everywhere in the signature. - Introduce a 2 stage generic resolver, that first walks the generic tree (since one generic can reference another), and then once that's flattened, produces the set of all possible concrete type signatures for a given generic signature - Introduce the ability for some types ("Parametric" ones) to be themselves generic across some T, or many T's, and resolve them recursively as well. - Once I've got every function typed, move this into a management command that can dump the entire signature set to a JSON struct, and then load that on startup, rather than doing the "compile" every time. We could also ship that json struct to other places if we want. - Start using the signature set to produce _much_ better error messages and hints in the query/filter editor - right now we only really check that the argument set is the right length, and don't hint much at all about the types your passing in, just handing off to clickhouse to generate the error message:

title: fix(capture): unroll request enum matching
body:
We're a little bit too clever by using a single enum to represent all possible event requests. It works great for writing the code, but it means if someone gives us invalid data, the event-specific parsing error gets swallowed by the enum matching failure, and the error message they get back is just:
```
failed to parse request: data did not match any variant of untagged enum RawRequest
```

With this change, the payload:
```json
{
  "api_key": "phc_thisisntarealposthogkeybutitpassescapture",
  "batch": [
    {
      "event": "test-event",
      "properties": {
        "distinct_id": "user_id",
        "property1": "value1"
      },
      "uuid": "wat",
      "timestamp": "2023-01-01T12:00:00.000Z"
    }
  ]
}
```

Instead results in the error message:
```
failed to parse request: invalid character: expected an optional prefix of `urn:uuid:` followed by [0-9a-fA-F-], found `w` at 1
```

Downsides/risks: We're disconnecting "what is a valid request to receive" from the declaration of the input data structure, which means if that struct definition changes, we'll need to go patch-up this parsing to account for that. I don't think this is a huge deal, as I'm fairly sure this data structure changes extremely rarely.

Obviously this is a pretty high-risk PR because it changes capture, as well.

title: feat(llma): Add cost anomaly counters to LLM analytics usage report

labels.name: team/llm-analytics
labels.description: LLM Analytics

body:
## Problem

Need to track potential data quality issues in LLM cost calculations and error rates for downstream analysis.

## Changes

Add new metrics to the `llm analytics usage` event:
- `total_ai_cost_usd_count`: count of events with non-null `$ai_total_cost_usd`
- `total_ai_cost_usd_negative_count`: count of events with negative cost
- `total_ai_cost_usd_zero_count`: count of events with zero cost
- `ai_is_error_count`: count of events where `$ai_is_error` = 'true'

Enables downstream calculations like:
- `total_ai_cost_usd_negative_count / total_ai_cost_usd_count` = % negative costs
- `total_ai_cost_usd_zero_count / total_ai_cost_usd_count` = % zero costs
- `ai_is_error_count / total_events` = % error rate

## How did you test this code?

Added test cases for cost anomaly counts and error count. Full test suite passes (15 tests).

## Changelog: (features only) Is this feature complete?

No - internal billing/analytics event, not user-facing.

title: fix: handle Array(UUID) columns in ArrowStream format conversion

labels.name: waiting
labels.description: Prevents stale-bot from marking the PR as stale.

body:
Closes #44622

Problem

ClickHouse cannot serialize UUID types in ArrowStream format, which results in errors like:

The type 'UUID' of a column 'person_ids' is not supported for conversion into Arrow data format

Both hogql_table implementations (run_workflow.py and materialize_view.py) already handled scalar UUID columns by converting them with toString().
However, array UUID columns (e.g. Array(UUID) such as person_ids on raw_sessions) were silently passed through without conversion, causing Arrow serialization failures.

Root Causes

  • run_workflow.py

    • A blanket and not is_array_type condition excluded all array types from conversion.

    • This made the existing arrayMap(x -> toString(x), col) branch (lines ~902–918) effectively dead code.

  • materialize_view.py

    • Array(UUID) columns were detected but incorrectly wrapped with toString(col) instead of using arrayMap.

    • The arrayMap conversion path was missing entirely.

Changes

  • Replaced the broad is_array_type exclusion with a new is_skippable_array helper:

    • Only skips arrays whose inner type is Arrow-compatible (e.g. Array(String), Array(Int64)).

  • Array(UUID), Array(IPv4), and Array(IPv6) are now correctly converted using:

    arrayMap(x -> toString(x), col)

  • Added explicit error handling for ClickHouse errors containing:

    is not supported for conversion into Arrow
    with a clearer, user-friendly message.

  • Added a regression test:

    • test_hogql_table_converts_array_uuid_columns_with_array_map

How did you test this code?

This PR was authored with AI assistance.

**Unit logic tests (all 16 cases pass):** | Type | Before | After | |------------------------------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------------------| | Array(UUID) | Passed through → Arrow error | arrayMap(x -> toString(x), col) | | Array(IPv4) / Array(IPv6) | Arrow error | arrayMap(x -> toString(x), col) | | Array(String) / Array(Int64) | Skipped | Skipped | | UUID (scalar) | toString(col) | toString(col) |

  • Added regression test will run in CI.

  • Full integration tests require a ClickHouse + PostgreSQL Docker setup and were not run locally.

Publish to changelog?

No

title: fix: fix mutation count metrics bug, bare excepts, and missing request timeout
body:
## Changes

### 🐛 Critical Bug Fix

**`clickhouse_mutation_count` — Prometheus metrics silently lost** (`posthog/tasks/tasks.py`)

The `for` loop that sets gauge metrics was **outside** the `with pushed_metrics_registry(...)` context manager due to incorrect indentation. This means mutation count metrics were created inside the context manager but populated **after** it exited, so they were **never pushed to the metrics gateway**.

This is a silent monitoring blind spot — alerting on ClickHouse mutations was effectively broken.

```python
# BEFORE (broken — loop outside context manager)
    with pushed_metrics_registry("celery_clickhouse_mutation_count") as registry:
        mutations_count_gauge = Gauge(...)
    for table, muts in rows:  # ← wrong indentation, outside `with`
        mutations_count_gauge.labels(table=table).set(muts)

# AFTER (fixed — loop inside context manager)
    with pushed_metrics_registry("celery_clickhouse_mutation_count") as registry:
        mutations_count_gauge = Gauge(...)
        for table, muts in rows:  # ← now correctly inside `with`
            mutations_count_gauge.labels(table=table).set(muts)
```

---

### 🔒 Security & Reliability Fixes

1. **`clickhouse_row_count`** — Used `QUERY.format(table=table)` string interpolation for ClickHouse queries instead of parameterized `%(table)s`. While table names come from a hardcoded constant, this violates SQL security guidelines. Fixed to use parameterized queries.

2. **`ingestion_lag`** — `requests.post()` had **no timeout**. If `SITE_URL` is unreachable, the Celery worker hangs **indefinitely**, starving the task queue. Added `timeout=10` and wrapped in try/except.

3. **`pg_row_count`** — Added null check on `cursor.fetchone()` result to prevent `TypeError`.

---

### ⚡ Bare `except:` → `except Exception:` (20+ locations, 11 files)

Bare `except:` catches `KeyboardInterrupt`, `SystemExit`, and `GeneratorExit` which prevents graceful shutdown of Celery workers, Temporal workers, and Django processes. Fixed across:

| File | Function/Context |
|------|-----------------|
| `posthog/tasks/tasks.py` | 5 monitoring tasks |
| `posthog/temporal/common/schedule.py` | `schedule_exists`, `a_schedule_exists` |
| `posthog/clickhouse/system_status.py` | `is_alive()` |
| `posthog/clickhouse/client/execute.py` | `format_sql()` |
| `posthog/async_migrations/runner.py` | `update_migration_progress()` |
| `posthog/async_migrations/setup.py` | `get_async_migration_definition()` → `KeyError` |
| `posthog/session_recordings/session_recording_helpers.py` | `snapshot_library_fallback_from()` |
| `posthog/session_recordings/session_recording_api.py` | `_distinct_id_from_request()` |
| `posthog/cdp/validation.py` | `InputsSerializer` → `(KeyError, TypeError, AttributeError)` |
| `posthog/temporal/data_imports/sources/mongodb/source.py` | `validate_credentials()` |
| `posthog/temporal/data_imports/sources/postgres/postgres.py` | `get_postgres_row_count()` |
| `posthog/temporal/data_imports/pipelines/pipeline/utils.py` | 4 locations in data pipeline |

---

### How did I test this?

- All changes are mechanical and behavior-preserving (except the mutation count fix which now correctly pushes metrics)
- No new logic introduced — only correcting exception handling scope
- Verified no syntax errors via editor diagnostics on all 12 files

### Does this work well for self-hosted?

Yes — all fixes improve reliability for both cloud and self-hosted deployments.

title: feat(sig): add report splitting flow
body:
Not sure about this one tbh. Current weaknesses:

- Doesn't look at existing reports when generating the new set - maybe these signals shouldn't have been grouped, but there's some _other_ reports in the DB that they should be part of. This might be partially fixed be re-running the signal ingestion flow on them, rather than this "make up a report directly" approach.
- I think it's way over-zealous on splitting. Lots of small issues in a roughly similar area should be grouped together, even if they're all technically "distinct". Right now, the "split" outcome has (with the test dataset of GH issues) been the _almost_ _the_ _only_ outcome (with one exception, interestingly).

I'm going to sit with it for a while, and see if I can come up with anything cleverer. I do think the "execute_child_workflow" change is needed though, to avoid some races. I want to work on parelleling activities within the workflows to improve throughput a bit, maybe.

title: feat: Add support for test/internal user filters to SQL editor
body:
## Problem

Users would like to use the test/internal user filter with SQL editor queries: https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/issues/20249.

## Changes

I've added support for test/internal filters to the SQL editor. I've added a backend condition so that queries will fail unless the select is from the `events` table. This felt like the cleanest way to handle this given the alternatives:

* return a warning that the filter is skipped e.g. for the `sessions` table - easy to miss this and get unexpected behavior
* adding support to other tables - way outside the scope of what I'm trying to do here

But there might be other options I've missed! If users want to combine e.g. an `events` table query with a `sessions` query, but still make use of `{filters}` for whatever reason, then they can still do this, but access filters directly - e.g. `SELECT id FROM sessions WHERE {filters.dateRange.from} > start_time` or whatever.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d20613be-3d9b-4389-9e8a-2593711c329b

## How did you test this code?

Unit tests + tested locally to make sure this is behaving as expected

## Publish to changelog?

Yes

title: feat: Consumer group implementation for batch exports
body:
## Problem

Currently, multiple consumers are only available for BigQuery batch export, and then only for a select number of users. Enabling this for all users is critical for us to be able to scale into the future and beyond.

However, we cannot enable multiple consumers for everyone due to a big issue with the current implementation: The number of batch exports consumers is hardcoded! This means that a batch export either uses all consumers or a single consumer. Thus, enabling this for everyone has the potential of being very wasteful when batch exports are small, as a small batch export can complete quickly with a single consumer.

<!-- Who are we building for, what are their needs, why is this important? -->

<!-- Does this fix an issue? Uncomment the line below with the issue ID to automatically close it when merged -->
<!-- Closes #ISSUE_ID -->

## Changes

This proposes a new `ConsumerGroup` protocol in the `consumer.py` module. A protocol was chosen so that each destination can decide how to initialize their own consumers without being constrained to a particular class. This particularly relevant as each consumer is different.

The protocol requires only the consumer setup and execution to be defined, and then takes care of:
* Polling consumers for their records processed.
* Using configured target duration and target size, calculate whether the current rate of consumption is enough to finish bytes left in duration left.
  * If not, add consumers.
  * TODO: If yes and there is capacity to spare, reduce consumers. This is not yet done as I've focused only on adding consumers.
* Every consumer task is wrapped in a `asyncio.TaskGroup`, so either all consumers succeed or they don't. This leaves no margin for partial failures. Since we work with a stage table, that's fine, we just drop the table and start fresh in a retry.

This PR *ONLY* has the `ConsumerGroup` protocol. Meaning it does not include the implementation of the protocol for each destination. That's coming up in a follow up. The implementation itself is pretty trivial though: Basically the `run_consumer_from_stage` function call changes to a `await group.run()` call, and the body of `run_consumer_from_stage` is divided among the `ConsumerGroup` protocol members.

~One tricky bit was left out of scope (precisely because its tricky/impossible): Estimating the size of an export to configure the group. The idea is to use historical data for each team for this: We can do a quick query to get the max(last 3 runs) or some other aggregation when starting the batch export and use that as the target size. It will not be perfect, but we can have a tight target duration (1/3 or 1/2 of an interval) so that if we underestimate we will scale to max consumers while the batch export has plenty of time to finish.~

PR has been updated to now use records exported rather than bytes exported. This is significantly easier to estimate accurately as we can use the results of the internal stage query.

<!-- If there are frontend changes, please include screenshots. -->
<!-- If a reference design was involved, include a link to the relevant Figma frame! -->

## How did you test this code?

Added unit tests for some parts of `ConsumerGroup`.

<!-- Briefly describe the steps you took. -->
<!-- Include automated tests if possible, otherwise describe the manual testing routine. -->

title: chore: Make primary key approach more consistent across sources
body:
## Problem

I've worked on a couple of tickets recently where customers have had trouble with incremental syncs, and they've ended up being related to primary keys and `id` fallbacks. I was looking at the logging in code, which led me down a bit of a rabbit hole understanding what the difference is in how we do incremental syncs between different sources.

I am very likely missing some context on why some of these differences exist in the first place!

https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C0A6L24BU0P/p1770995891536829

## Changes

Generally make primary key management for incremental syncs more consistent in terms of:
- logging
- fallback to `id` in the absence of primary keys - and make it clearer that we are doing so
- achieved via a common `get_primary_keys` utility function to share the logging/logic for this across sources

Source specific changes:
- Ensure that we check primary key uniqueness for BigQuery, Redshift, and Snowflake, where uniqueness isn't enforced by the  database
- Added duplicate primary key check for MSSQL, MySQL and Snowflake, but only on `id`, and only when being used as a fallback)
- Added `id` column fallback to Snowflake, which was missing

I'll also update our docs to make this behavior clearer if this change looks good/like something we'd want

## How did you test this code?

Setup BigQuery and Snowflake sources for testing locally - might need to verify it for other sources too? I think there's also a risk this breaks some syncs given we're adding checks - but those would be for sources using incremental syncs that _could_ result in duplicates in PostHog.

title: feat(bots): implement known bot IP and CIDR range detection
body:
## Problem

The `isKnownBotIp` function was failing to match IP addresses within CIDR subnet ranges. The bot IP list (`nodejs/assets/bot-ips.txt`) contains ~1,400 CIDR notation entries (e.g., `185.32.187.0/24`, `2a02:6b8::/29`), but the implementation only performed strict string equality checks using `Array.prototype.includes()`.

This meant bot detection was bypassed for any crawler originating from a subnet range rather than a single static IP, significantly reducing the effectiveness of bot filtering in data pipelines and transformations.

Closes #47204

## Changes

**Modified files:**
- `nodejs/src/cdp/hog-transformations/bots/bots.ts` - Pre-parse IP list into exact matches (Set) and CIDR ranges at module load time
- `nodejs/src/cdp/hog-transformations/transformation-functions.ts` - Implement network-aware IP matching using ipaddr.js
- `nodejs/src/cdp/hog-transformations/transformation-functions.test.ts` - Add comprehensive test coverage for CIDR matching (both IPv4 and IPv6)

**Implementation details:**
1. Split bot IP list into two data structures at module initialization:
   - `KNOWN_BOT_IP_SET`: Set of exact IP addresses for O(1) lookups
   - `KNOWN_BOT_CIDR_RANGES`: Array of pre-parsed CIDR ranges
2. Updated `isKnownBotIp()` to:
   - First check exact matches (fast path)
   - Then validate against CIDR ranges using `ipaddr.js` (already a dependency)
   - Handle both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses
   - Gracefully handle malformed IPs and CIDR entries

## How did you test this code?

**Automated tests:**
```bash
cd nodejs && pnpm test -- transformation-functions.test.ts
```

All 16 tests pass, including:
- Exact IP matches from the bot list
- IPv4 addresses within various CIDR ranges (/24, /27)
- IPv4 addresses outside CIDR ranges
- IPv6 addresses within and outside CIDR ranges
- Invalid input handling (non-strings, null, undefined)

**Manual verification:**
```bash
# Verified bot-ips.txt contains 1,399 CIDR entries
grep -c '/' nodejs/assets/bot-ips.txt

# Confirmed ipaddr.js correctly parses sample CIDR ranges
node -e "const ipaddr = require('ipaddr.js'); console.log(ipaddr.parseCIDR('185.32.187.0/24'))"
```

No docs update needed - this is an internal bug fix that makes the existing API work as intended.

👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._

## Publish to changelog?

No - this is a bug fix for internal functionality, not a user-facing feature.

Bumps [webpack](https://github.com/webpack/webpack) from 5.88.2 to 5.104.1.
Release notes

Sourced from webpack's releases.

v5.104.1

5.104.1

Patch Changes

  • 2efd21b: Reexports runtime calculation should not accessing WEBPACK_IMPORT_KEY decl with var.
  • c510070: Fixed a user information bypass vulnerability in the HttpUriPlugin plugin.

v5.104.0

5.104.0

Minor Changes

  • d3dd841: Use method shorthand to render module content in __webpack_modules__ object.
  • d3dd841: Enhance import.meta.env to support object access.
  • 4baab4e: Optimize dependency sorting in updateParent: sort each module only once by deferring to finishUpdateParent(), and reduce traversal count in sortWithSourceOrder by caching WeakMap values upfront.
  • 04cd530: Handle more at-rules for CSS modules.
  • cafae23: Added options to control the renaming of at-rules and various identifiers in CSS modules.
  • d3dd841: Added base64url, base62, base58, base52, base49, base36, base32 and base25 digests.
  • 5983843: Provide a stable runtime function variable __webpack_global__.
  • d3dd841: Improved localIdentName hashing for CSS.

Patch Changes

  • 22c48fb: Added module existence check for informative error message in development mode.
  • 50689e1: Use the fully qualified class name (or export name) for [fullhash] placeholder in CSS modules.
  • d3dd841: Support universal lazy compilation.
  • d3dd841: Fixed module library export definitions when multiple runtimes.
  • d3dd841: Fixed CSS nesting and CSS custom properties parsing.
  • d3dd841: Don't write fragment from URL to filename and apply fragment to module URL.
  • aab1da9: Fixed bugs for css/global type.
  • d3dd841: Compatibility import.meta.filename and import.meta.dirname with eval devtools.
  • d3dd841: Handle nested __webpack_require__.
  • 728ddb7: The speed of identifier parsing has been improved.
  • 0f8b31b: Improve types.
  • d3dd841: Don't corrupt debugId injection when hidden-source-map is used.
  • 2179fdb: Re-validate HttpUriPlugin redirects against allowedUris, restrict to http(s) and add a conservative redirect limit to prevent SSRF and untrusted content inclusion. Redirects failing policy are rejected before caching/lockfile writes.
  • d3dd841: Serialize HookWebpackError.
  • d3dd841: Added ability to use built-in properties in dotenv and define plugin.
  • 3c4319f: Optimizing the regular expression character class by specifying ranges for runtime code.
  • d3dd841: Reduce collision for local indent name in CSS.
  • d3dd841: Remove CSS link tags when CSS imports are removed.

v5.103.0

Features

  • Added DotenvPlugin and top level dotenv option to enable this plugin
  • Added WebpackManifestPlugin
  • Added support the ignoreList option in devtool plugins
  • Allow to use custom javascript parse function

... (truncated)

Changelog

Sourced from webpack's changelog.

5.104.1

Patch Changes

  • 2efd21b: Reexports runtime calculation should not accessing WEBPACK_IMPORT_KEY decl with var.
  • c510070: Fixed a user information bypass vulnerability in the HttpUriPlugin plugin.

5.104.0

  • 4baab4e: Optimize dependency sorting in updateParent: sort each module only once by deferring to finishUpdateParent(), and reduce traversal count in sortWithSourceOrder by caching WeakMap values upfront.
  • 04cd530: Handle more at-rules for CSS modules.
  • cafae23: Added options to control the renaming of at-rules and various identifiers in CSS modules.
  • d3dd841: Added base64url, base62, base58, base52, base49, base36, base32 and base25 digests.
  • 5983843: Provide a stable runtime function variable __webpack_global__.
  • d3dd841: Improved localIdentName hashing for CSS.

Patch Changes

  • 22c48fb: Added module existence check for informative error message in development mode.
  • 50689e1: Use the fully qualified class name (or export name) for [fullhash] placeholder in CSS modules.
  • d3dd841: Support universal lazy compilation.
  • d3dd841: Fixed module library export definitions when multiple runtimes.
  • d3dd841: Fixed CSS nesting and CSS custom properties parsing.
  • d3dd841: Don't write fragment from URL to filename and apply fragment to module URL.
  • aab1da9: Fixed bugs for css/global type.
  • d3dd841: Compatibility import.meta.filename and import.meta.dirname with eval devtools.
  • d3dd841: Handle nested __webpack_require__.
  • 728ddb7: The speed of identifier parsing has been improved.
  • 0f8b31b: Improve types.
  • d3dd841: Don't corrupt debugId injection when hidden-source-map is used.
  • 2179fdb: Re-validate HttpUriPlugin redirects against allowedUris, restrict to http(s) and add a conservative redirect limit to prevent SSRF and untrusted content inclusion. Redirects failing policy are rejected before caching/lockfile writes.
  • d3dd841: Serialize HookWebpackError.
  • d3dd841: Added ability to use built-in properties in dotenv and define plugin.
  • 3c4319f: Optimizing the regular expression character class by specifying ranges for runtime code.
  • d3dd841: Reduce collision for local indent name in CSS.
  • d3dd841: Remove CSS link tags when CSS imports are removed.

title: fix(billing): Refresh org before applying usage quota limiting

labels.name: bug
labels.description: Something isn't working right

body:
The cron job loads all organizations at the start and iterates through them over potentially 30+ minutes. If a billing update arrives during that time, the stale data from the initial load would overwrite the fresh billing data when saved.

title: Join with persons table to check for next_join for LIMIT
body:
## Problem

When creating Data warehouse joins with the persons table on the left side of the join, the generated ClickHouse query incorrectly applies a LIMIT 101/102 to the inner persons subquery. This causes joins to fail or return zero results when matching records exist beyond the first ~100 persons.

This PR refers to https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/issues/43404


## Changes
 
select_from_persons_table applies several optimizations intended only for direct persons queries:
 WHERE-clause pushdown via WhereClauseExtractor
 LIMIT / ORDER BY pushdown for argMax v2
However, these optimizations were applied even when persons was part of a JOIN (node.select_from.next_join), which caused the inner persons subquery to be truncated and broke joins.
So,fixing persons joins by disabling LIMIT/WHERE optimizations when joins are present

## How did you test this code?

Confirmed Limit not applicable for Joins for query with Persons table

## Publish to changelog?

No

title: chore(deps): bump esbuild from 0.25.10 to 0.25.11

body:
Bumps [esbuild](https://github.com/evanw/esbuild) from 0.25.10 to 0.25.11.
Release notes

Sourced from esbuild's releases.

v0.25.11

  • Add support for with { type: 'bytes' } imports (#4292)

    The import bytes proposal has reached stage 2.7 in the TC39 process, which means that although it isn't quite recommended for implementation, it's generally approved and ready for validation. Furthermore it has already been implemented by Deno and Webpack. So with this release, esbuild will also add support for this. It behaves exactly the same as esbuild's existing binary loader. Here's an example:

    import data from './image.png' with { type: 'bytes' }
const view = new DataView(data.buffer, 0, 24)
const width = view.getInt32(16)
const height = view.getInt32(20)
console.log('size:', width + '\xD7' + height)

  • Lower CSS media query range syntax (#3748, #4293)

    With this release, esbuild will now transform CSS media query range syntax into equivalent syntax using min-/max- prefixes for older browsers. For example, the following CSS:

    @media (640px <= width <= 960px) {
  main {
    display: flex;
  }
}

    will be transformed like this with a target such as --target=chrome100 (or more specifically with --supported:media-range=false if desired):

    @media (min-width: 640px) and (max-width: 960px) {
  main {
    display: flex;
  }
}
Changelog

Sourced from esbuild's changelog.

0.25.11

  • Add support for with { type: 'bytes' } imports (#4292)

    The import bytes proposal has reached stage 2.7 in the TC39 process, which means that although it isn't quite recommended for implementation, it's generally approved and ready for validation. Furthermore it has already been implemented by Deno and Webpack. So with this release, esbuild will also add support for this. It behaves exactly the same as esbuild's existing binary loader. Here's an example:

    import data from './image.png' with { type: 'bytes' }
const view = new DataView(data.buffer, 0, 24)
const width = view.getInt32(16)
const height = view.getInt32(20)
console.log('size:', width + '\xD7' + height)

  • Lower CSS media query range syntax (#3748, #4293)

    With this release, esbuild will now transform CSS media query range syntax into equivalent syntax using min-/max- prefixes for older browsers. For example, the following CSS:

    @media (640px <= width <= 960px) {
  main {
    display: flex;
  }
}

    will be transformed like this with a target such as --target=chrome100 (or more specifically with --supported:media-range=false if desired):

    @media (min-width: 640px) and (max-width: 960px) {
  main {
    display: flex;
  }
}

title: refactor(hogql): move or_null logic to function metadata
body:
## Changes
This removes the hardcoded `ADD_OR_NULL_DATETIME_FUNCTIONS` list from `config.py` and moves the behavior to the `HogQLFunctionMeta` definition.

## Rationale
The previous implementation relied on a "magic list" in `config.py` to determine if a function should be wrapped in `assumeNotNull`. This refactor moves that configuration to the function definition itself (`use_or_null_variant=True`), which cleans up technical debt and centralizes function configuration.

## Verification
- Updated `core.py` to accept the new flag.
- Updated `conversions.py` and `datetime.py` to use the flag.
- Updated `printer/base.py` to consume the flag.
- Verified via `pytest posthog/hogql/test/test_printer.py`.

body:
**What:** Bumps Playwright from 1.45.0 to 1.58.2 (latest stable) across all references and switches E2E tests to the new Chromium headless mode.

**Changes**
- Bump `playwright` and `@playwright/test` to 1.58.2 in root and playwright package.json
- Bump Playwright Docker images in `Dockerfile.playwright` and `ci-storybook.yml`
- Switch E2E chromium project to `channel: 'chromium'` (new headless mode — uses real Chromium instead of chrome-headless-shell)
- Add rendering determinism flags (`--disable-font-subpixel-positioning`, `--force-color-profile=srgb`, `--disable-lcd-text`) for consistent screenshots
- Mask dynamic regions (nav sidebar, billing banner) in workflow screenshot tests
- Fix CI retry regex to detect Playwright 1.58's screenshot error format (ANSI codes broke the old pattern)

**Why new headless:** The old `chrome-headless-shell` is a stripped-down binary that doesn't match real browser behavior. `channel: 'chromium'` launches full Chromium in headless mode — closer to what users actually see. Storybook stays on the default engine since its test-runner abstracts browser launch and the component-level diffs don't benefit.

title: fix: Guard against `group` being undefined
body:
## Problem

Fixes https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/issues/45900.

This was causing a crash when `group` was `undefined` after PostHog AI pushed some changes to an insight.

## Changes

Guard against `group` being undefined, but also use a consistent ID for the logic across renders.

I haven't checked other usage of `TaxonomicPopover` to check whether they'd benefit from the same fix, but did go down the rabbit hole of checking for similar usage of `taxonomicFilterLogicKey`. The existing `useMemo` usage here caused some warnings about it not being guaranteed between renders, so I've switched to use `useId` instead. This would need us to upgrade `@types/react` to match the version of React we're using - but there's a load of type warnings I didn't want to fix, so I've added some `@ts-expect-error`s for my usage instead.

Feedback welcome!

title: feat(hogql): push events predicate down transform (attempt No 2)
body:
## Problem

For below SQL:

```sql
SELECT event, session.$session_duration
FROM events
WHERE timestamp >= '2024-01-01' AND timestamp < '2024-01-08'
```

HogQL generates below ClickHouse query:

```sql
SELECT events.event AS event, events__session.`$session_duration` AS `$session_duration` 
    events LEFT JOIN (
  SELECT dateDiff(%(hogql_val_5)s, min(toTimeZone(raw_sessions.min_timestamp, %(hogql_val_6)s)), max(toTimeZone(raw_sessions.max_timestamp, %(hogql_val_7)s))) AS `$session_duration`, raw_sessions.session_id_v7 AS session_id_v7 
  FROM raw_sessions 
  WHERE and(equals(raw_sessions.team_id, 420), greaterOrEquals(fromUnixTimestamp(intDiv(toUInt64(bitShiftRight(raw_sessions.session_id_v7, 80)), 1000)), minus(%(hogql_val_8)s, toIntervalDay(3))), lessOrEquals(fromUnixTimestamp(intDiv(toUInt64(bitShiftRight(raw_sessions.session_id_v7, 80)), 1000)), plus(%(hogql_val_9)s, toIntervalDay(3)))) 
  GROUP BY raw_sessions.session_id_v7, raw_sessions.session_id_v7) AS events__session ON equals(toUInt128(accurateCastOrNull(events.`$session_id`, %(hogql_val_10)s)), events__session.session_id_v7) 
  WHERE and(equals(events.team_id, 420), greaterOrEquals(toTimeZone(events.timestamp, %(hogql_val_11)s), %(hogql_val_12)s), less(toTimeZone(events.timestamp, %(hogql_val_13)s), %(hogql_val_14)s), equals(events.event, %(hogql_val_15)s), isNotNull(events.`$session_id`)) 
  LIMIT 50000
```

ClickHouse does not properly push down the predicates through a JOIN, therefore we the above query would in some cases scan hundreds of TB of events.

## Changes

Figure out the predicates on events table and put events in subquery:

```sql
  SELECT events.event AS event, events__session.`$session_duration` AS `$session_duration` 
  FROM (
  SELECT events.`$session_id` AS `$session_id`, events.event AS event, toTimeZone(events.timestamp, %(hogql_val_0)s) AS timestamp 
  FROM events 
  WHERE and(equals(events.team_id, 420), and(greaterOrEquals(toTimeZone(events.timestamp, %(hogql_val_0)s), %(hogql_val_1)s), less(toTimeZone(events.timestamp, %(hogql_val_2)s), %(hogql_val_3)s), equals(events.event, %(hogql_val_4)s), isNotNull(events.`$session_id`)))) AS events LEFT JOIN (
  SELECT dateDiff(%(hogql_val_5)s, min(toTimeZone(raw_sessions.min_timestamp, %(hogql_val_6)s)), max(toTimeZone(raw_sessions.max_timestamp, %(hogql_val_7)s))) AS `$session_duration`, raw_sessions.session_id_v7 AS session_id_v7 
  FROM raw_sessions 
  WHERE and(equals(raw_sessions.team_id, 420), greaterOrEquals(fromUnixTimestamp(intDiv(toUInt64(bitShiftRight(raw_sessions.session_id_v7, 80)), 1000)), minus(%(hogql_val_8)s, toIntervalDay(3))), lessOrEquals(fromUnixTimestamp(intDiv(toUInt64(bitShiftRight(raw_sessions.session_id_v7, 80)), 1000)), plus(%(hogql_val_9)s, toIntervalDay(3)))) 
  GROUP BY raw_sessions.session_id_v7, raw_sessions.session_id_v7) AS events__session ON equals(toUInt128(accurateCastOrNull(events.`$session_id`, %(hogql_val_10)s)), events__session.session_id_v7) 
  WHERE and(equals(events.team_id, 420), greaterOrEquals(toTimeZone(events.timestamp, %(hogql_val_11)s), %(hogql_val_12)s), less(toTimeZone(events.timestamp, %(hogql_val_13)s), %(hogql_val_14)s), equals(events.event, %(hogql_val_15)s), isNotNull(events.`$session_id`)) 
  LIMIT 50000
```

It decreases number of read events rows from **103B** to **80K**.

My previous attempt: https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/45535

The change is enabled when a modifier: **`pushDownPredicates == true`**.

## How did you test this code?

Added tests.

Run test queries manually.

## Publish to changelog?

Improved HogQL queries selecting events and lazy loaded tables. For affected queries, it improves the query execution time 1000x. It should also allow to run queries that were failing before.

title: chore(dev): consolidate env vars, auto-resolve 1Password secrets

labels.name: waiting
labels.description: Prevents stale-bot from marking the PR as stale.

body:
**Problem:** Dev env vars scattered across `bin/start`, `bin/start-backend`, and `bin/start-celery` with duplicates. AI developers need to manually wrap commands with `op run --env-file=.env.local` for 1Password secrets.

**Solution:**
- `.env.development` (committed) with all static dev defaults
- `.env.local` for user overrides + 1Password secrets (gitignored)
- Auto-detect `op://` refs in `.env.local` and resolve via 1Password CLI
- `hogli run <cmd>` for running any command with the same env resolution

**1Password usage:**

Create `.env.local` with your secrets:
```
OPENAI_API_KEY=op://Private/OpenAI/api-key
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=op://Private/Anthropic/api-key
```

Then just run `hogli start` or `hogli run ./manage.py shell` - secrets resolve automatically.

**Secret enforcement:**

`.env.secrets` lists vars that must come from 1Password. If you set these without proper `op://` refs in `.env.local`, `bin/start` refuses to run.

title: chore: add Rust workspace versioning guide
body:
## Problem

The Rust workspace lacks a documented process for updating the Rust toolchain and dependencies. This creates several issues:

  - Inconsistent updates: Different team members may update Rust/dependencies at different times without coordination
  - Version drift: Rust version synchronization points (CI workflow, Dockerfile, MSRV in Cargo.toml) can fall out of sync
  - No clear cadence: Without a schedule, updates are either neglected (security/performance risk) or applied too eagerly (stability risk)
  - Onboarding friction: New contributors don't know when or how to perform updates

Rust stable releases every 6 weeks, making version management a recurring concern that benefits from standardization.
## Changes

Introduces `rust/VERSIONING.md` - a protocol document that defines:

  - Update cadence: 12-week cycle, adopting the previous even minor version when an odd version hits stable (e.g., upgrade to 1.94.0 when 1.95.0 releases)
  - Synchronization points: Explicit list of files/lines that must be updated together (CI workflow, Dockerfile, MSRV)
  - Step-by-step procedure: 13-step process from checking current versions through creating the PR
  - Dependency strategy: Use cargo outdated --root-deps-only to focus on direct dependencies, with guidance on evaluating breaking changes
  - Quick reference: Copy-paste command sequence for experienced developers

The "one version behind" strategy provides stability by letting the community surface issues in the latest release before we adopt it.
## How did you test this code?

<!-- Briefly describe the steps you took. -->
<!-- Include automated tests if possible, otherwise describe the manual testing routine. -->

<!-- Docs reminder: If this change requires updated docs, please do that! Engineers are the primary people responsible for their documentation. 🙌 -->

👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._

## Publish to changelog?

<!-- For features only -->

<!-- If publishing, you must provide changelog details in the #changelog Slack channel. You will receive a follow-up PR comment or notification. -->

<!-- If not, write "no" or "do not publish to changelog" to explicitly opt-out of posting to #changelog. Removing this entire section will not prevent posting. -->

title: test(signals): Integration tests for video summarization
body:
## Problem

Our new session video summarization #43805, set to be a core part of proactive tasks, came without automated tests for early iteration velocity. But now we have the approach mostly down and in prod, so before unflagging this, worth to have tests in place.

## Changes

This PR adds a suite of tests for each of the steps of video summarization. It would have taken me ages to hand-roll all of these, so in fact ALL are Claude-made. Each is eye-reviewed by me for soundness though. What matters most in the end is codifying the current behavior, to prevent future regressions.

Here are the two prompts I used for initial creation and verification:

1.
    > Implement unit/integration tests for each of the steps of our new video-based session summarization workflow at  @posthog/temporal/ai/session_summary/activities/. Each activity should have its own test_ file, and then the workflow as a whole should have one too. We want integration tests primarily, i.e. ones using real ClickHouse/Redis, like most other tests in PostHog. (Unit tests are good for trickier isolated functions.) For inspiration, see how we test other Temporal workflows.    
2.
    > Validate that every assumption that the tests within @posthog/temporal/ai/session_summary/activities/tests/ make is correct. Go test-by-test and think critically about user needs vs. our production code.

(yes, I'm now taking feedback on my prompts, what has the world come to)

title: feat(hog-function): implement batch retry for failed invocations
body: Closes #35640 ## Changes This pull request introduces a new feature for batch retrying failed Hog Function invocations within a specified date range, making it easier for users to recover from errors in bulk. The changes span backend API, frontend UI, and task management logic, providing a complete end-to-end solution for scheduling and executing batch retries. **Batch Retry Feature for Hog Functions** _Backend API and Task Implementation:_ * Added a new `batch_retry` API endpoint to `HogFunctionViewSet` that queues a Celery task to retry all failed invocations for a Hog Function in a given date range and status. * Implemented the `batch_retry_hog_function` Celery task in `posthog/tasks/hog_functions.py` to: * Query log entries for failed invocations, * Extract related event IDs, * Fetch event data from ClickHouse, * Trigger retries by constructing the necessary payload and calling `create_hog_invocation_test`. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-d2ccf699716f7fe0563673552368cf8caa08e2515bb2e18341bc53b4b1845b87R113-R341) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-d2ccf699716f7fe0563673552368cf8caa08e2515bb2e18341bc53b4b1845b87L11-R16) _Frontend UI and Logic:_ * Added a "Retry failures in range" button to the Hog Function logs UI, allowing users to trigger the batch retry flow via a confirmation dialog. * Integrated the new `batchRetry` API method into the frontend, and exposed the `retryBatch` action in the `hogFunctionLogsLogic` logic, which calls the backend and shows toast notifications for progress and success/failure. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-fc0647beab64e4906eb6a581b0476dba8073601854e616d8ca3345539b0412b8R3218-R3227) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-db9a85603dfc5d1f419f5e8a5ff6142b084d8d45b7bb2ceb51911bdf930be3a4R113) [[3]](diffhunk://#diff-db9a85603dfc5d1f419f5e8a5ff6142b084d8d45b7bb2ceb51911bdf930be3a4R306-R326) _Logic and Data Flow Enhancements:_ * Connected `logEntryParams` to the logic to ensure correct date range and status parameters are used for batch retry actions. These changes collectively provide a robust mechanism for users to easily schedule and execute batch retries of failed Hog Function invocations, improving error recovery and operational efficiency. ## Screenshots ### Master branch 👇 ### After the implementation 👇 👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._

title: feat(oauth): add checksums to refresh tokens

labels.name: waiting
labels.description: Prevents stale-bot from marking the PR as stale.

body:
We currently store these in plaintext (the default for django-oauth-toolkit). Even though they are high entropy (and so wouldn’t affect other applications if one was leaked), we want to reduce the impact that unauthorised access to this table could affect.

This adds checksums for refresh tokens (already present for access tokens) and adds a migration to backfill existing refresh tokens, it also adds logic to query based on the checksum. I will drop the token field in a subsequent PR.

title: feat(llma): add evaluation results API endpoint

labels.name: team/llm-analytics
labels.description: LLM Analytics

body:
## Problem

Evaluation results (`$ai_evaluation` events) are stored in ClickHouse but there's no REST API endpoint to query them. The frontend uses inline HogQL queries, and the MCP server would need to embed raw HogQL too. This is fragile — if the event schema changes, every consumer breaks.

## Changes

Add `GET /api/environments/:id/llm_analytics/evaluation_results/` endpoint that queries `$ai_evaluation` events from ClickHouse using HogQL AST (safe parameterization).

**Query params:**
- `evaluation_id` — filter by evaluation UUID
- `generation_id` — filter by generation event UUID
- `result` — filter by status: `pass`, `fail`, or `na`
- `limit` — max results (default 50, max 200)

At least one of `evaluation_id` or `generation_id` is required.

**Returns:** `{ results: [...], count: N }` with each result containing:
- `uuid`, `timestamp`, `evaluation_id`, `evaluation_name`
- `generation_id`, `trace_id`
- `result` (bool/null), `reasoning`, `applicable`

Includes unit tests with mocked ClickHouse queries.

**Part 2 of a 3-PR stack** — depends on PR #50004, MCP tool follows in PR #50011.

## How did you test this code?

- Unit tests in `test_evaluation_results.py` (mocked ClickHouse)
- Follows existing patterns from `evaluation_summary.py`
- Uses HogQL AST for safe query construction (no f-strings with user data)

Closes #50025 (tracked)

title: feat(replay): add console log text search to AI session replay filters
body:
## Summary

- Adds `LogEntryPropertyFilter` to the `MaxUniversalFilterValue` union type used by the AI assistant's session replay filtering tool, enabling `log_entry` type filters (console log level and message text search)
- Updates MCP tool prompts with documentation and examples for console log text filtering (e.g., searching for "IP detection error" or "something went wrong" in session logs)
- Adds schema validation tests confirming `log_entry` filters are accepted in `MaxRecordingUniversalFilters`

### Root cause

The `MaxUniversalFilterValue` type (used by the `filter_session_recordings` AI tool) only included `EventPropertyFilter`, `PersonPropertyFilter`, `SessionPropertyFilter`, `RecordingPropertyFilter`, `GroupPropertyFilter`, and `MaxRecordingEventFilter`. The `LogEntryPropertyFilter` type was missing, so the AI agent could not create filters with `type: "log_entry"` — causing validation errors when users asked to filter replays by console log content.

The backend query layer already fully supports `log_entry` filters with both `key: "level"` and `key: "message"`. This was purely a schema gap in the AI tool's allowed filter types + missing prompt documentation.

### Changes

| File | What changed |
|------|-------------|
| `frontend/src/queries/schema/schema-assistant-replay.ts` | Added `LogEntryPropertyFilter` to import and `MaxUniversalFilterValue` union; updated `MaxInnerUniversalFiltersGroup` JSDoc |
| `frontend/src/queries/schema.json` | Regenerated from TypeScript (includes `LogEntryPropertyFilter` in `MaxUniversalFilterValue`) |
| `posthog/schema.py` | Regenerated from schema.json (includes `LogEntryPropertyFilter` in `MaxInnerUniversalFiltersGroup.values`) |
| `products/replay/backend/prompts.py` | Added console log text search documentation, examples for message filtering and combined level+message filtering |
| `ee/hogai/tools/replay/filter_session_recordings.py` | Added console log properties documentation to tool description |
| `ee/hogai/tools/replay/test/test_filter_session_recordings.py` | Added parameterized schema validation tests for log_entry filters |

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify `LogEntryPropertyFilter` is accepted in `MaxRecordingUniversalFilters` schema (new tests in `TestFilterSessionRecordingsToolSchema`)
- [ ] Test AI assistant can create console log text search filters (ask Max: "find sessions with 'something went wrong' in console logs")
- [ ] Verify existing console log level filtering still works
- [ ] Verify combined level + message filtering works (e.g., error logs containing specific text)

title: feat: allow admins to rescue ownerless organizations
body:
## Summary

- Allow organization admins to promote any member (including themselves) to owner when the organization has no active owner
- Allow owners to leave the organization when other active owners exist (previously blocked unconditionally)
- Show a warning banner in the organization members settings when no owner is present, guiding admins to use the actions menu

## Problem

When an organization's owner leaves the company and their account is deactivated, remaining members are stuck in a deadlock:
- Only owners can promote someone to owner
- No active owner exists to perform the promotion
- This blocks SOC2 compliance and general org management

## How it works

**Backend (`posthog/models/organization.py`):**
- `validate_update()` now detects "ownerless rescue" conditions: the target level is Owner, the requesting user is at least Admin, and no active owner exists in the org
- In this case, both the self-level-change restriction and the owner-only-promotion restriction are bypassed

**Backend (`posthog/models/user.py`):**
- `User.leave()` now allows owners to leave when other active owners exist, enabling proper ownership transfer workflows

**Frontend (`permissioning.ts`, `Members.tsx`, `membersLogic.tsx`):**
- `getReasonForAccessLevelChangeProhibition()` accepts a new `organizationHasOwner` parameter
- `membersLogic` adds an `organizationHasOwner` selector derived from loaded members
- Members page shows a warning banner when no owner is detected

## Test plan

- [x] Backend: admin can promote another member to owner when no active owner exists
- [x] Backend: admin can self-promote to owner when no active owner exists
- [x] Backend: admin cannot promote to owner when an active owner already exists
- [x] Backend: admin can promote to owner when the only owner is deactivated
- [x] Backend: regular members cannot promote even when no active owner exists
- [x] Backend: owner can leave when other active owners exist
- [x] Backend: sole owner cannot leave
- [x] Frontend: permissioning utility tests for ownerless rescue scenarios
- [ ] Manual: verify warning banner appears for admins in an ownerless org
- [ ] Manual: verify "Make owner" action appears for admins in ownerless org (including on own row)

title: fix(replay): handle session property filters in recording queries
body:
## Summary

- **Root cause:** Web Analytics sends `SessionPropertyFilter` (type="session") properties like `$entry_current_url`, `$entry_pathname`, `$channel_type` via `RecordingsQuery.properties` when users switch domains or navigate dashboards. The backend's `_strip_person_and_event_and_cohort_properties` function only strips event, person, group, and cohort types — session properties remain as "unexpected", triggering `capture_exception(UnexpectedQueryProperties(...))` ~262 times.
- **Fix:** Routes session properties through the events subquery (consistent with how event, person, and group properties are already handled) and adds `is_session_property` to the strip function so they no longer trigger the exception.

### Changes

1. **`posthog/session_recordings/queries/utils.py`**: Added `is_session_property()` helper and included it in `_strip_person_and_event_and_cohort_properties`
2. **`posthog/session_recordings/queries/sub_queries/events_subquery.py`**: Added `session_properties` property and processing in `_get_queries_for_matching`, `_having_predicates`, and `_has_negative_properties`
3. **Test**: Added regression test verifying session property filters don't trigger `UnexpectedQueryProperties`

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify the new test `test_session_property_filter_does_not_trigger_unexpected_query_properties` passes in CI
- [ ] Navigate Web Analytics dashboards and switch domains — recording queries should succeed without `UnexpectedQueryProperties` exceptions
- [ ] Verify existing session recording filtering still works (event, person, group, cohort filters)

title: feat(workflows): support encrypted inputs in workflows
body:
### Summary                                                   
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
  - Workflows now support destinations with secret (encrypted) inputs like Meta Ads, TikTok Ads, etc.
  - Previously, the frontend filtered out any destination template with secret: true input fields from the workflow dispatch list because there was no secure storage mechanism for secrets on the HogFlow model                                                                                                           
  - Adds an encrypted_inputs field to HogFlow (using the same EncryptedJSONStringField / Fernet encryption as HogFunction) that stores secret inputs keyed by action ID                                                                                                                                                    
  - On save, move_secret_inputs() separates secret values from actions[].config.inputs into encrypted_inputs based on each template's inputs_schema, mirroring the pattern used by realtime destinations                                                                                                                   
  - The API never returns decrypted secrets — only {"secret": true} markers, same as HogFunction                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
  - The Node.js side decrypts encrypted_inputs at load time and passes them through to HogInputsService.buildInputs() at execution time via the existing encrypted_inputs merge path                                                                                                                                       
  - Draft saves also handle secret extraction to avoid plaintext secrets in the draft JSON field                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
 ### Changes                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
  Django:

title: Add optional OpenAPI validation for E2E runs (Django middleware, Playwright collector & reporter, CI summary)
body:
### Motivation

- Surface OpenAPI schema mismatches observed during end-to-end tests without failing test execution so teams can triage API contract problems. 
- Aggregate per-request validation findings from browser-driven tests and surface them in CI artifacts and the GitHub Actions step summary. 
- Provide an opt-in mechanism so validation can be enabled for CI E2E runs without impacting normal test behavior.

### Description

- Add a Django middleware `posthog/openapi_validation_middleware.py` that non-blockingly validates `/api/*` requests/responses against the generated OpenAPI schema and sets `X-PostHog-OpenAPI-Validation-Errors` on responses. 
- Wire up configuration in `posthog/settings/web.py` (`OPENAPI_E2E_VALIDATION_*`) and add the middleware to `MIDDLEWARE`. 
- Add Playwright-side collection and attachment logic in `playwright/utils/openapi-validation.ts`, attach per-test summaries in `playwright/utils/playwright-test-core.ts`, and add a reporter `playwright/reporters/openapi-validation-reporter.ts` to aggregate results into `openapi-validation-summary.json`. 
- Make Playwright conditional reporter and header injection configurable in `playwright/playwright.config.ts`, add README docs (`playwright/README.md`) describing how to enable the feature, and add the `openapi-core` dependency to `pyproject.toml`. 
- Add a focused unit test `posthog/test/test_openapi_validation_middleware.py` that verifies middleware enabling, header gating, and non-blocking reporting of request/response errors. 
- Update the GitHub Actions workflow `/.github/workflows/ci-e2e-playwright.yml` to enable the feature in CI via env vars and add a step that summarizes the `openapi-validation-summary.json` into the GitHub Actions step summary and Playwright comment aggregation.

### Testing

- Ran the new middleware unit tests with `python -m pytest posthog/test/test_openapi_validation_middleware.py` and they passed. 
- Playwright reporter and CI summary generation are exercised when `PLAYWRIGHT_OPENAPI_VALIDATE=1` / `OPENAPI_E2E_VALIDATION_ENABLED=1` are set in E2E runs; no additional automated tests for the reporter were added in this PR.

------
[Codex Task](https://chatgpt.com/codex/tasks/task_e_69a8a2ba81f0832eb8c0df0d15286943)

title: fix(feature-flags): filter group flag default insights to only show events with groups set
body:
## Problem
When a feature flag targets groups (e.g. companies), the auto-generated 
default insights show all $feature_flag_called events — including ones 
fired without any group context. This inflates the `false` evaluation 
count and makes rollout percentages appear incorrect and confusing.

Fixes #39135

## Changes
- Added `_get_default_flag_properties()` helper in `dashboard_templates.py`
  that appends a `$groups is_set` event filter when `aggregation_group_type_index`
  is not None
- Both default insights now set `aggregation_group_type_index` on the source
- Fixed `_update_tile_with_new_key` which had a `len(values) == 1` guard
  that silently skipped key updates for group flags (now handles 1 or 2 values)

## How did you test this code?
- Added 3 new tests in `TestFeatureFlag`:
  - `test_create_feature_flag_dashboard_for_group_flag`
  - `test_create_feature_flag_dashboard_for_user_flag`
  - `test_updating_feature_flag_key_for_group_flag`
- Updated existing `test_create_feature_flag_usage_dashboard` to match
  new behavior
- Manually verified by creating a group-targeted flag via API locally
  and inspecting the generated dashboard insights

title: feat: add billing usage change email toggle to notification settings

labels.name: feature/billing
labels.description: Feature Tag: Billing

body:
## Summary

- Adds a per-user toggle in **Settings > Notifications** to opt out of "We've detected a change in your usage" (spike detection) billing emails
- Stores the preference as `billing_usage_change_emails` in the user's `notification_settings`
- Syncs the preference to the billing service via the `org_users` payload so it can filter recipients when sending spike emails
- Triggers an immediate billing sync when the preference changes so opt-out takes effect right away

## How it works

1. User toggles "Billing usage change alerts" off in Settings > Notifications
2. PostHog saves `billing_usage_change_emails: false` in their `partial_notification_settings`
3. PostHog immediately calls `update_billing_organization_users()` for all the user's orgs, including the new preference per user in the `org_users` payload
4. The billing service (separate PR in posthog/billing) reads this preference and skips opted-out users when sending `billing_usage_spike_detected` emails

## Test plan

- [ ] Toggle appears in Settings > Notifications under "Billing usage change alerts"
- [ ] Toggling off sends `PATCH /api/users/@me/` with `billing_usage_change_emails: false`
- [ ] Backend test: `pytest posthog/api/test/test_user.py -k notification`
- [ ] Billing manager test: `pytest ee/billing/test/test_billing_manager.py -k update_billing_organization_users`

title: fix(data-warehouse): skip pw validation when editing source config
body:
## Problem

When editing a Postgres source's configuration (e.g. just changing the description), the form blocks submission because the password field is empty and marked as required. The API intentionally omits password fields from responses for security, and the backend already preserves existing passwords when none is provided in an update, but the frontend form validation doesn't account for this, forcing users to re-enter the password even for unrelated changes.

## Changes

- Added an `isUpdateMode` parameter to `getErrorsForFields` that skips required validation for `password`-type fields when editing an existing source
- Passed `isUpdateMode: true` from the source configuration update form
- Added 3 test cases covering: password required in create mode, password skipped in update mode, non-password fields still required in update mode

## How did you test this code?

I verified the fix with automated tests:

- All 21 tests in `sourceWizardLogic.test.ts` pass, including 3 new ones that cover the update mode behavior
- Existing backend tests (`test_update_with_null_password_preserves_existing`, `test_update_with_empty_string_password_preserves_existing`) confirm the backend already handles password preservation correctly

But, I haven't tested this manually.

## Publish to changelog?

No

title: feat: HogQL object-level access control

labels.name: waiting
labels.description: Prevents stale-bot from marking the PR as stale.

body:
## Problem

The resource-level PR (#47130) removes entire system tables from users who lack resource access. But users with resource-level access can still see all rows, including objects they've been explicitly denied access to via object-level `AccessControl` entries (e.g., a specific dashboard set to "none" for a user).

## Changes

- Adds object-level access control to the HogQL printer. 
- When querying system tables, a `WHERE toString(id) NOT IN (...)` clause is injected to exclude specific objects the user can't access. 
- The same logic is applied as in the existing access control (although it is not possible to reuse it)
  - The highest access level from object default, role, or member entries applies
  - Org admins bypass the check entirely

**How it works:**
Building on the resource-level PR which stores `user_access_control` on the Database instance:

1. ClickHousePrinter.visit_join_expr() calls _ensure_access_control_where_clause() for each system PostgresTable
2. `build_access_control_guard()` in `printer/access_control.py`
3. The guard is combined with the existing team_id guard in `visit_join_expr()`, for example:
```sql
SELECT * FROM postgresql('...', 'posthog_dashboard', '...')
WHERE equals(team_id, 123)
  AND notIn(toString(id), ('42', '789'))
LIMIT 100
```

## How did you test this code?
Tests + manually via SQL editor
Under hogql-access-control feature flag

## Publish to changelog?
Not yet

title: idea: Added team filters bytecode for use everywhere
body:
## Problem

Was chatting about a small-time feature around internal test filters that we might want to add to event processing.

Currently we have the issue that every time team filters change, we have to re-generate all hog function bytecodes. This is both expensive to do and confusing as it can break in hard to understand ways.

If we generated it when saving, we could look it up in processing rather than duplicating it to _every hog function_ that uses it as well as have the ability to show when a person edits it what the limitations are.


## Changes

- [x] Adds the bytecode and error to the model
- [x] No backfill yet
- [x] Sets an error if it fails (e.g. due to cohort lookups)
- [ ] Adds a warning to the UI if the configured filters will not work in real time processing contexts (flags, workflows, destinations)
- [ ] TODO: Figure out if this makes sense and modify hog function processing to lookup the filter rather than including it in their own bytecode (then remove it from being dynamically added to the bytecode)

title: fix(docker): build Unit 1.35.0 ourselves
body:
Motivation

- Ensure production images include the Django 5 ASGI compatibility fix from Unit, even if Docker Hub tags lag upstream releases.
- Reduce CI/network overhead by avoiding a full-history clone of the Unit repository.
- Improve maintainability by removing duplicated ./configure flag blocks.

Description

- Keep the runtime base image on unit:1.34.2-python3.12.
- Build Unit from upstream source using:
- Use a shallow clone (--depth 1 --branch "$UNIT_GIT_TAG") to fetch only the required release ref.
- Verify reproducibility by asserting the checked-out HEAD matches UNIT_GIT_REF.
- Deduplicate configure options by defining a shared CONFIGURE_ARGS string and reusing it for both configure invocations (unitd and Python module).

title: feat(mcp): implement CRUD operations for annotation management
body:
##Problem

When using the PostHog MCP to manage dashboards and insights, it's useful to also create annotations to mark significant events (feature releases, experiments started, etc.) directly from the MCP without switching to the UI.

Currently annotations can only be managed via the UI.

Closes #44873

## Changes

Added five new MCP tools for Annotations CRUD operations:

- **annotation-create** - Create new annotations to mark significant events with content and date markers
- **annotation-list** - List all annotations with pagination and scope filtering support
- **annotation-get** - Fetch specific annotation by ID
- **annotation-update** - Update annotation properties (content, date_marker, scope)
- **annotation-delete** - Soft delete annotations via PATCH request

## How did you test this code?

Tested all Annotations CRUD operations via MCP server using curl:

**Setup:** 
- Started MCP dev server (`services/mcp/typescript && pnpm dev`)
- Initialized MCP session with JSON-RPC 2.0 protocol
- Configured PostHog API token for authentication
### Test Results

| Operation | Status | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| **Create** | ✅ Success | Created annotation returned annotation ID |
| **List** | ✅ Success | Retrieved all annotations with pagination and scope filtering |
| **Get** | ✅ Success | Fetched annotation by ID, verified all metadata fields |
| **Update** | ✅ Success | Updated content with resolution status, `updated_at` timestamp changed |
| **Delete** | ✅ Success | Soft deleted via PATCH with `deleted: true` |

All five CRUD operations completed successfully with proper HTTP status codes and response schema validation.
