title: fix(web-analytics): remove non-existent columns from pre-aggregated property mappings
body:
## Summary

- Remove `$geoip_time_zone`, `$geoip_country_name`, `$browser_version`, and `$os_version` from `BASE_SUPPORTED_PROPERTIES` — these properties were mapped to columns that don't exist in the `web_pre_aggregated_stats` / `web_pre_aggregated_bounces` ClickHouse tables
- Remove `$geoip_time_zone` from the frontend `WEB_ANALYTICS_PRE_AGGREGATED_ALLOWED_EVENT_PROPERTIES` allow list
- Tighten `can_use_preaggregated_tables()` to use an allowlist (`event`, `session`) instead of a denylist (`cohort`), preventing person or other unexpected filter types from enabling the pre-aggregated path

## Problem

When users filtered by properties like `$geoip_time_zone` (which was exposed in the filter UI for pre-aggregated mode), the system attempted to query non-existent columns on pre-aggregated tables. This caused query errors instead of gracefully falling back to raw event tables.

The `can_use_preaggregated_tables()` check only rejected `cohort` type filters but allowed any other type through, including `person` filters that can't work on pre-aggregated tables.

These issues are contributing to the query errors and timeouts reported on the Web Analytics dashboard, particularly with the `web-analytics-filters-v2` feature preview enabled.

## How did you test this code?

Added parameterized tests verifying that:
- `$geoip_time_zone`, `$browser_version`, `$os_version`, and `$geoip_country_name` filters correctly disable pre-aggregated tables
- Person property filters correctly disable pre-aggregated tables

## Test plan

- [ ] Existing pre-aggregated table tests pass (property filtering, breakdown consistency)
- [ ] New tests verify unsupported properties cause correct fallback to raw events
- [ ] New test verifies person property filters cause correct fallback
- [ ] Cohort filter tests in `test_web_analytics_cohort_filter.py` still pass

title: feat(web-analytics): Add Web Analytics Digest Email Job

labels.name: update-snapshots
labels.description: Enable auto commit snapshots for Storybook and Playwright

body: ## Problem Users have no way to get an overview of this site's analytics week to week. ## Changes Adds new email job that queries stats and send the email to opted-in users. ## How it works Org Discovery: 1. Loads all orgs then filters by `web-analytics-weekly-digest` feature flag 2. Fan out one Dagster op per qualifying org Per Org: 1. Check email is available 2. Load org memberships then filter by feature flag at user level 3. Load all teams then build team digest for each Queries: - WebOverviewQuery - WebStatsTableQuery - WebStatsTableQuery - WebGoalsQuery Per User: 1. Check if the user is opted in to notifications 2. Filter teams by user access permissions 3. If user hasn't configured project preferences, pick the project with most visitors 4. Apply per-project notification settings, split into enabled/disabled lists 5. Skip if no enabled projects Delivery: 1. Sort enabled projects by visitor count 2. Send email with campaign key wa_digest_{org}_{user}_{YYYY-WW} for de-dupe ## How did you test this code? 👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._ ## Publish to changelog? ## Docs update

title: feat(flags): paired schedule presets and edit UI for scheduled changes

labels.name: update-snapshots
labels.description: Enable auto commit snapshots for Storybook and Playwright

body:
## Problem

Users scheduling recurring feature flag changes (e.g. "enable at 9am weekdays") often need a complementary schedule (e.g. "disable at 5pm weekdays") but must create each one manually. Additionally, there is no way to edit an existing scheduled change — users must delete and recreate.

## Changes

![2026-03-31 13 28 43](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/08f8353b-719a-477f-8586-d3733dad9115)


**Paired schedule presets** — the Repeats dropdown now includes a "Paired schedules" option group (Business hours, Weekdays only, Custom pair) when the operation is "Change status". Selecting a preset creates two complementary enable/disable cron schedules in one action. This is purely frontend — the backend sees two standard create calls.

**Edit scheduled changes** — each active schedule card now has an edit (pencil) button that opens a `LemonModal` pre-populated with the schedule's current values. Users can change the date/time, repeat interval or cron, end date, and enable/disable status. The edit logic lives in a separate `featureFlagScheduleEditLogic.ts` to isolate state from the creation form. Only changed fields are PATCHed.

**Shared `describeCron` helper** — extracted the duplicated cron-to-human-string logic into a single exported function used across all three files.

Key details:
- Paired presets: `business_hours` (enable 9am / disable 5pm Mon-Fri), `weekdays_only` (enable midnight Mon / disable end-of-day Fri), `custom_pair` (user-provided crons)
- Partial failure handling: if the enable schedule succeeds but the disable fails, a warning toast explains the situation
- Edit modal validates: future date for one-time schedules, 5-field cron syntax, end date after start
- Editing a paused schedule does not silently unpause it (uses derived is_recurring comparison)

## How did you test this code?

All 80 existing `featureFlagLogic` tests pass. TypeScript compiles without new errors. Frontend formatter passes. This is a frontend-only change — no backend modifications. I'm an agent and have not performed manual browser testing.

👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._

## Publish to changelog?

no

## Docs update

No docs update needed — this builds on the existing scheduled changes feature.

## 🤖 LLM context

This PR was co-authored with Claude Code. The implementation follows a 5-stage plan (paired presets logic → paired presets UI → edit logic → edit UI → polish). Code review feedback was incorporated before the PR: fixed a bug where editing a paused schedule would silently unpause it, and extracted a shared `describeCron` helper to eliminate 5x duplication.

title: feat(sql): copy as markdown
body:
## Problem

Building this for _**me**_! And hopefully some others too.. Typically when dealing with support requests it's really helpful to share the results of a SQL query at the time of writing. The best way to share small snippets of data tends to be in the format of a markdown table.

This is an export feature of TablePlus that I always really valued, so would be nice to bring this to PostHog

## Changes

Adds a new method to copy data as markdown, which flattens and escapes requisite characters to make a markdown table. This is based on the same table extraction as we use for CSV so that the data is flattened in the right way for a simple table view.

The value copied to the clipboard would look like this, noting the header `a|b` has the pipe escaped:

```table
| a\|b                                 | created_at                  | event                |
| ------------------------------------ | --------------------------- | -------------------- |
| 019ca6b5-3074-0000-2158-87da4bda20bc | 2026-03-01T00:03:38.228896Z | $autocapture         |
| 019ca774-cc5e-0000-5d43-b5e07887068c | 2026-03-01T03:32:55.518299Z | $autocapture         |
| 019ca76b-ed43-0000-ad25-730b45f9d19a | 2026-03-01T03:23:14.115933Z | $feature_flag_called |
| 019ca725-12c3-0000-e995-732da5dbad96 | 2026-03-01T02:05:50.659183Z | $feature_flag_called |
| 019ca6c3-5569-0000-9d71-81000f41c845 | 2026-03-01T00:19:05.193179Z | $feature_flag_called |
```

Whilst the columns don't need to be padded, it make reading it much easier in places where markdown is not supported, but monospaced values are.

## How did you test this code?

Tested locally and in unit testing

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9b6766f4-aafd-4f9b-ad77-0a65aad36038

title: Add insights-create tool for creating insights from query dicts
body:
## Problem

Users need a way to create insights directly from query objects (dicts) via the MCP tools, complementing the existing `insight-create-from-query` tool which saves queries as insights. This enables more flexible insight creation workflows.

## Changes

- Added new `insights-create` MCP tool (`createFromDict.ts`) that accepts a query object as a JSON dict and creates an insight from it
- Extended the API client with `createFromDict` method to handle insight creation from query dicts
- Added `CreateInsightFromDictInputSchema` and `CreateInsightFromDictInput` type to the insights schema
- Added `InsightCreateFromDictSchema` to tool inputs schema
- Registered the new tool in the tool map
- Updated tool definitions in both `tool-definitions.json` and `tool-definitions-all.json` with metadata for the new `insights-create` tool

The new tool accepts:
- `name`: Insight name
- `query`: Query object as a JSON dict (any valid PostHog query structure)
- `description`: Optional description
- `favorited`: Boolean flag
- `tags`: Optional array of tags

Returns the created insight with a URL to access it.

## How did you test this code?

Code structure follows existing patterns from `insight-create-from-query` tool. The implementation:
- Reuses validated schema definitions
- Follows the same error handling and response patterns
- Integrates with existing API client infrastructure
- Properly typed with TypeScript

## Publish to changelog?

Yes - this adds a new user-facing MCP tool capability.

title: feat(feature-flags): mixed person+group blast radius
body:
## Problem

Feature flags currently require all condition sets to use the same aggregation type (all person or all group). This prevents flags that need mixed targeting, such as "50% of enterprise companies OR 75% of beta users" in a single flag.

Closes #33206

## Changes

**Backend** (`user_blast_radius.py`, `feature_flag.py`):
- `BlastRadiusResult` dataclass replaces the raw tuple return, with optional `groups_affected`, `total_groups`, and per-query error fields
- Group-aggregated conditions return `users_affected=0, total_users=0` with group counts populated, cleanly separating person and group blast radius responses
- Removed validation blocking mixed aggregation types across condition sets, gated behind the `feature-flag-mixed-targeting` feature flag
- Flag-level `aggregation_group_type_index` is derived from condition sets: all-same → that value, mixed → None (for backward compatibility)
- Per-condition Early Access Feature and circular dependency checks

**Frontend** (`featureFlagReleaseConditionsLogic.ts`, `FeatureFlagReleaseConditionsCollapsible.tsx`):
- Per-condition "Targeting" dropdown lets users set different group types per condition set (gated behind `feature-flag-mixed-targeting`)
- `taxonomicGroupTypesForCondition` selector enforces homogeneous condition sets — group-aggregated conditions only show group properties, person-aggregated only show person/cohort properties
- Blast radius display shows group counts for group-aggregated conditions, user counts for person-aggregated conditions
- Each condition sends its own `aggregation_group_type_index` to the blast radius endpoint

## How did you test this code?

- 33 backend tests covering: mixed condition sets allowed, per-condition aggregation normalization, pure person/group blast radius, cohort+group separate conditions, `$group_key` filters, and error field absence on success
- 28 frontend logic tests including condition-level aggregation routing and group count storage from blast radius responses
- Snapshot update for group blast radius query

👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._

## Publish to changelog?

no

## Docs update

No docs changes needed.

## 🤖 LLM context

Co-authored with Claude Code. The implementation follows a staged plan: backend mixed property handling → condition-level aggregation → display mixed blast radius → edge cases and per-condition UI. All changes are gated behind the `feature-flag-mixed-targeting` feature flag.

title: chore(devex): speed up hogli start cold boot
body:
## Problem

`hogli start` cold boot is slow. The ingestion/nodejs processes pay ~30s for an unconditional `cargo build --release` of cyclotron (which rarely changes), Rust compilation is limited to 1 CPU core, and docker images are checked against the registry on every start.

## Changes

- **Cyclotron build skip**: `prestart:dev` now checks if `index.node` is newer than source files before running `cargo build --release`. Skips in ~0.3s when up to date (vs ~30s before).
- **CARGO_BUILD_JOBS**: Default increased from 1 to `ncpu/2` (capped at 4). The cargo file lock only serializes linking, not per-crate compilation.
- **`--no-pull` flag**: `bin/start --no-pull` uses `--pull missing` instead of `--pull always` for docker-compose. Default behavior unchanged.

## How did you test this code?

- Verified cyclotron skip: `pnpm build:cyclotron:if-needed` prints "up to date" and completes in 0.3s when no source changes; correctly rebuilds when a `.rs` file is touched or `index.node` is missing
- Verified CARGO_BUILD_JOBS calculation: 12 cores -> 4 jobs
- Verified generated mprocs.yaml contains `${DOCKER_PULL_POLICY:-always}` shell substitution
- All 202 hogli tests pass

## Publish to changelog?

No

title: fix(billing): Turn usage signals for SFDC into an incremental pipeline
body: 1. StartToClose timeout: The fetch_salesforce_org_ids_activity had a 2-minute timeout that was too short for ~280k org mappings as data volume grew. 2. Event loop blocking: Gzip decompression and JSON parsing of large Redis payloads (~280k entries) ran on the async event loop, blocking heartbeats and causing Temporal to consider the activity dead. 3. Unbounded gRPC payloads: The entire org-mappings dataset was passed through Temporal's gRPC layer as a single activity result, risking the 4 MB limit. Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) ## Problem Fix an error with the [usage_enrichment data pipeline](https://cloud.temporal.io/namespaces/posthog-prod-us.usz2o/workflows/salesforce-usage-enrichment-schedule-2026-03-29T06%3A00%3A00Z/019d382d-98f9-71ff-88ec-f24bab75ab9c/timeline) ## Changes - Paginated workflow with Continue-As-New: Replace the monolithic fetch-then-process loop with a page-at-a-time approach. Each workflow execution processes one page of org mappings via enrich_org_page_activity, then calls continue_as_new to keep event history bounded. - Redis List for org mappings: Switch from a single gzip-compressed blob to a Redis List (RPUSH/LRANGE) so pages can be read via LRANGE without decompressing the entire dataset. Uses pipelined EXISTS + LRANGE to avoid TOCTOU races. - Consolidated activity: Merge the old fetch_salesforce_org_ids_activity and update_salesforce_usage_activity into a single enrich_org_page_activity that reads from Redis, aggregates signals, and updates Salesforce — keeping all heavy data inside the activity and off the gRPC layer. - Thread-offloaded decompression: Move gzip.decompress + json.loads into asyncio.to_thread to avoid blocking the event loop (still used for the accounts cache path). - Timing instrumentation: Add time.monotonic measurements for Redis reads to aid future diagnosis. - Bump timeouts: Cache activity → 10 min, enrich page activity → 30 min with 5-min heartbeat. ## How did you test this code? - added unit tests - ran pipeline locally (not fully completed) 👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._ ## Publish to changelog? no ## Docs update

title: feat(llma): restrict trial users to cost-reasonable models
body:
## Problem

Trial users (no BYOK API key) could access all supported models including expensive pro/opus tiers like `gpt-5.2-pro`, `o3-pro`, `o1-pro`, and `claude-opus-4-6`. Since PostHog pays for trial API calls, this creates significant cost exposure. The issue affected both the playground (model picker showed all models, completion endpoint had no restriction) and evaluations (a separate `POSTHOG_ALLOWED_MODELS` dict existed but wasn't enforced server-side).

## Changes

- Add `TRIAL_MODELS` allowlist to each provider config (`OpenAIConfig`, `AnthropicConfig`, `GeminiConfig`) with cost-reasonable models plus one flagship per provider for quality evaluation
- Introduce a `PROVIDERS` registry in the LLM module so adding a new provider is a single-line change — `TRIAL_MODEL_IDS`, `TRIAL_MODELS_BY_PROVIDER`, `get_trial_models()`, and `get_default_models()` all derive from it
- Update `GET /api/llm_proxy/models/` to return only trial-eligible models when no provider key is given
- Enforce the allowlist on `POST /api/llm_proxy/completion/` — returns 403 for non-trial models without a provider key
- Enforce the allowlist in `execute_llm_judge_activity` when no provider key is set, with `model_not_allowed` as a skippable error that disables the evaluation
- Remove the separate `POSTHOG_ALLOWED_MODELS` dict from `model_configuration.py` and unify with the new trial model lists

**Trial models per provider:**
- **OpenAI:** gpt-4.1, gpt-4.1-mini, gpt-4.1-nano, gpt-5-mini, gpt-5-nano, o3-mini, o4-mini
- **Anthropic:** claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-haiku-4-5
- **Gemini:** gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-flash-lite, gemini-2.0-flash, gemini-2.0-flash-lite, gemini-1.5-flash

BYOK users are unaffected — they continue to see all models from their provider API.

## How did you test this code?

I'm an agent and haven't tested this manually. Automated test coverage:

- 4 unit tests in `TestTrialModelAllowlist` validating allowlist config (subset checks, dynamic expensive model exclusion, trial-only model set)
- 4 HTTP-level tests in `TestTrialModelEnforcement` exercising the 403 enforcement on the completion endpoint, the 200 allow path, and the models list endpoint returning only trial models
- 1 async test in `TestRunEvaluationWorkflow` verifying the eval activity rejects non-trial models with a non-retryable `model_not_allowed` error

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

No docs update needed — this is a backend enforcement change with no user-facing API changes.

title: feat(mcp): results formatting for lifecycle queries
body: ## Problem When the AI assistant executes a `LifecycleQuery`, results fall back to raw JSON serialization because no dedicated formatter exists. This produces verbose, unoptimized output that wastes context tokens and is harder for the LLM to reason about compared to the compact pipe-delimited format used by trends, funnels, and retention formatters. ## Changes - **New `LifecycleResultsFormatter`** (`ee/hogai/context/insight/format/lifecycle.py`): formats lifecycle query results into a compact pipe-delimited matrix with columns `Date|New|Returning|Resurrecting|Dormant`. Groups by event series — single series omits the header, multi-series separates sections with `Event:` headers. Handles missing statuses (fills `0`), custom event names, and datetime formatting. - **Registered in dispatcher** (`format/__init__.py`): routes `AssistantLifecycleQuery | LifecycleQuery` to the new formatter. - **Integrated in query executor** (`query_executor.py`): added to `is_supported_query`, `_compress_results`, and `get_example_prompt`. - **Added `LIFECYCLE_EXAMPLE_PROMPT`** (`prompts.py`): describes the output format so the LLM understands the table structure. - **Added `AssistantLifecycleQuery` to `AnyAssistantGeneratedQuery`** (`utils/types/base.py`). Example output for a single-series lifecycle query: ``` Date|New|Returning|Resurrecting|Dormant 2025-10-01|6936|29541|13263|-16735 2025-11-01|7101|30794|12662|-18946 2025-12-01|7929|31093|13783|-19464 ``` ## How did you test this code? Added unit tests in `ee/hogai/context/insight/format/test/test_lifecycle.py` covering: - Single series formatting - Multi-series formatting with `Event:` headers - Empty results - Custom event names via `action.custom_name` - Missing lifecycle statuses (partial data) - Datetime with timezone offset formatting I am an agent and have not tested this manually beyond code-based unit tests. ## Publish to changelog? No ## 🤖 LLM context This PR was authored by Claude Code. Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_015Bs4c1HwZYzWweztsh7LLY

title: feat(phai): exclude large AI properties from data schema scanning
body:
## Problem

Large AI properties like `$ai_input`, `$ai_output_choices`, `$ai_input_state`, and `$ai_output_state` contain raw model input/output data that can be extremely large. When the event taxonomy query runner scans these properties for sample values, it can cause performance issues and return unhelpful data. These properties should still be listed as available, but their sample values should not be scanned.

## Changes

### Core Changes (event_taxonomy_query_runner.py)
- Added `AI_LARGE_PROPERTIES_BY_EVENT` dictionary to define which properties should be excluded from scanning, scoped by event type (`$ai_span`, `$ai_generation`, `$ai_embedding`)
- Added `_get_excluded_large_properties()` method to retrieve excluded properties for the current event
- Modified `_get_omit_filter()` to exclude large properties from the HogQL regex filter using anchored patterns to avoid partial matches
- Modified `_get_subquery()` to filter out excluded properties from scanning while still allowing them to be listed
- Added special handling in `_calculate()` to append excluded properties with empty sample values so they appear in property listings

### Downstream Changes
- **read_taxonomy/core.py**: Added `_is_excluded_ai_property()` helper and updated `execute_taxonomy_query()` to return a user-friendly message when sample values are requested for excluded properties
- **query_planner/toolkit.py**: Updated `retrieve_event_or_action_property_values()` to return a user-friendly message for excluded properties

## How did you test this code?

This change modifies the event taxonomy query logic to exclude large properties from scanning. The implementation:
- Uses regex anchors to ensure exact property name matching (avoiding false positives)
- Preserves excluded properties in the results with empty sample values
- Provides user-friendly messages in the AI agent tools when these properties are queried
- Handles edge cases like when all requested properties are excluded (returns a dummy query)

The changes are straightforward filtering logic that integrates with existing query building patterns. Existing tests should continue to pass as the core query structure remains unchanged.

## Publish to changelog?

No

title: feat(llma): filter non-AI spans from OTEL ingestion pipeline
body:
## Problem

When users configure PostHog's OTEL trace exporter with frameworks like Next.js (via `NodeSDK` + `PostHogTraceExporter`), the SDK sends *all* OpenTelemetry spans — HTTP requests, fetch calls, middleware, etc. — not just AI-related ones. These irrelevant spans get ingested as `$ai_span` events, polluting LLM analytics data and wasting quota.

## Changes

Server-side filtering in the Rust capture service so only spans with recognized AI attributes are ingested:

- `get_event_name` now returns `Option<&str>` — unrecognized spans yield `None` and are skipped
- `has_ai_attributes_raw` checks relevance directly on raw protobuf `KeyValue` attributes before any JSON conversion, avoiding wasted allocations for irrelevant spans
- Raw span count capped at 1,000 before expansion to prevent DoS from oversized payloads
- AI span count still capped at 100 for the actual event limit
- New `capture_ai_otel_spans_filtered` counter metric tracks how many non-AI spans are being dropped
- Marker list covers Vercel AI SDK (`ai.operationId`, `ai.telemetry.functionId`), Traceloop/OpenLLMetry (`traceloop.*`, `llm.request.type`), Pydantic AI/Logfire (`pydantic_ai.*`, `logfire.*`), and generic `gen_ai.*` semantic conventions

The filtering intentionally errs on the permissive side — a false positive (non-AI span ingested as `$ai_span`) is harmless, while a false negative (real AI span dropped) loses data.

## How did you test this code?

- Unit tests for `get_event_name` covering all classifier paths, marker fallback, and `None` for irrelevant spans
- Unit test for `expand_into_events` verifying irrelevant spans are skipped
- Integration tests: `test_irrelevant_http_spans_are_ignored` (all-HTTP batch returns 200 with no events), `test_mixed_requests_only_emit_relevant_ai_spans` (mixed batch only emits AI spans)
- Existing tests updated to include AI marker attributes so they continue producing events
- All tests pass, clippy clean

I'm an agent and have not tested this manually beyond the automated test suite.

## Publish to changelog?

no

title: feat(mcp): add mcpcat observability tracking to MCP service
body:
## Problem

The MCP service needs better observability and analytics tracking for tool calls and server operations. The previous custom analytics implementation was tightly coupled to tool registration logic and lacked comprehensive tracing capabilities.

Additionally, the initial mcpcat integration (drafted by @rafaeelaudibert in #50588) used the `identify` callback as a workaround to attach per-request metadata (session ID, client info, region, org/project IDs) as `userData` — which mcpcat treated as person properties via `$set`, not event properties. This meant metadata wasn't directly queryable on events in PostHog.

MCPCat has since shipped v0.1.15 with proper `eventTags`/`eventProperties` callbacks and a PostHog AI observability integration (`enableAITracing`), built in collaboration with the PostHog team. This PR replaces #50588 with a version that uses those new capabilities.

Closes #50588

## Changes

- **Added `mcpcat` dependency (^0.1.15)** for standardized MCP observability
- **Replaced custom analytics tracking** with mcpcat's built-in observability features
- **Removed manual event tracking code** from tool registration handlers
- **Created `mcpcat.ts` module** with identity provider interface and initialization logic
- **Separated metadata into proper callbacks:**
  - `identify` — returns only `userId` + identity-related person properties (`region`, `organization_id`, `project_id`)
  - `eventTags` — string metadata spread as top-level event properties (`$session_id`, `mcp_client_name`, `mcp_client_version`, `mcp_protocol_version`, `region`, `organization_id`, `project_id`)
  - `eventProperties` — flexible JSON for non-string/long values (`mcp_version`, `client_user_agent`)
- **Enabled `enableAITracing: true`** on the PostHog exporter — emits native `$ai_span` events for every tool call, populating `$ai_session_id`, `$ai_trace_id`, `$ai_span_id`, `$ai_span_name`, `$ai_latency`, `$ai_input_state`, `$ai_output_state`, and `$ai_is_error`
- **Added comprehensive test coverage** for identify, eventTags, and eventProperties callbacks

## How did you test this code?

Added unit tests for the mcpcat integration covering:
- Proper initialization with correct configuration (including `enableAITracing` and new callbacks)
- Identity resolution with person properties (region, org, project)
- eventTags callback returning all string metadata, skipping undefined values
- eventProperties callback returning numeric/long-string metadata, skipping undefined values
- Graceful handling when identity providers fail
- Resilient initialization that doesn't break server startup

All 9 tests pass. TypeScript typecheck passes with no errors.

## Publish to changelog?

No — this is an internal observability infrastructure change.

## Additional context

The MCPCat team ([@naji247](https://github.com/naji247)) worked with @rafaeelaudibert to build the `eventTags`/`eventProperties` and `enableAITracing` features in mcpcat ([MCPCat/mcpcat-typescript-sdk#32](https://github.com/MCPCat/mcpcat-typescript-sdk/pull/32)), then prepared this PR to integrate them into PostHog's MCP server. See the [MCPCat + PostHog Integration Guide](https://github.com/MCPCat/mcpcat-typescript-sdk/pull/32) for full details on the new capabilities.

title: Feat: support realtime cohort evaluation in hogfunction/hogflow filters
body:
## Problem

<!-- Who are we building for, what are their needs, why is this important? -->

<!-- Does this fix an issue? Uncomment the line below with the issue ID to automatically close it when merged -->
<!-- Closes #ISSUE_ID -->
Addresses part of #50020. People want to trigger workflows or destinations based on whether the user that triggered the workflow/destination is part of a cohort or not. While the HogVM functions “inCohort” and “notInCohort” are supported by the compiler, they could previously not be executed by the CDP event evaluation path .

## Changes
CDP event evaluation path can now resolve inCohort and notInCohort functions. The functions are provided as sync functions to the execHog() by pre-fetching cohort ids from db before actual bytecode execution. Pre-fetching the cohort ids only happens if the bytecode contains "inCohort"/"notInCohort". 

I considered implementing the same pattern used by the hogflow executor to allow async functions but decided for this less drastic change as its impact is more predictable for a hot path like the CDP events consumer.

**What's addressed:**
- **CohortMembershipResolver** (`nodejs/src/utils/cohort-membership-resolver.ts`): New utility that batch-resolves cohort memberships from Postgres `cohort_membership` table using `LazyLoader` for automatic batching and caching
- filterFunctionsInstrumented now allows to be provided with filterFunctionProviders - each can provide a synchronous function for precheck (shouldResolve) and an actual resolve-Function that's run if the precheck passes. That way HogExecutorService and HogFlowExecutorService can pass a cohort function provider which injects inCohort / notInCohort as VM functions. 

**What's NOT addressed:**
- The **precalculated filters pipeline** (`CdpRealtimeCohortsConsumer` / `CdpCohortMembershipConsumer`), **RealtimeSupportedFilterManagerCDP** (cohort-type filters)
- from the original issue I figured that the cohort_membership should be treated as the source of truth regarding cohort membership. During local testing I noticed that the initial calculation of the cohort after creation of it does not populate this table, so I had to trigger an event that adds the person to the cohort after I created the cohort itself. Unless I lack some context here, that should also be addressed for the feature to work reliably.

## How did you test this code?
### Automatic tests
- Unit tests for `CohortMembershipResolver` (batching, empty results, missing persons)
- Added tests for hog-function-filtering changes : filterFunctionProviders, parameterized inCohort/notInCohort scenarios etc

### Manually tested as follows:
- Create a cohort xyz with condition “has fired event xyz”
- Create user A and user B, fire event xyz from user B
- Create a workflow, replace its trigger bytecode via DB with:
["_H", 1,
  32, "workflow-trigger", 32, "event", 1, 1, 11,
  33, 5, 2, "inCohort", 1,
  3, 2]
- Fire “workflow-trigger” event for user A and B -> workflow fires only for event sent by user B
- Replace “inCohort” from the workflow with “notInCohort”
- Fire “workflow-trigger” event from user A and B -> workflow fires only for event sent by user A

The same testing approach was used to test the hog function invocation path.

## Publish to changelog?
No, as no user-noticeable changes are made.

title: refactor: prepare codebase for Django 5.0 compatibility
body:
## Summary

Applies `django-upgrade --target-version 5.0` across the entire Python codebase to prepare for Django 5.0.

**46 files changed** — +90 / -108 (net -18 lines). All changes are mechanical and backward-compatible with Django 4.2.

## Migration Report

### What changed

Two mechanical patterns applied across 46 files:

#### 1. `SomeEnum.choices` → `SomeEnum` (13 model files)

Django 5.0 accepts enum classes directly for `choices=` ([docs](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/5.0/releases/5.0/#enumerations-types)):

```python
# Before (Django 4.2)
session_recording_retention_period = models.CharField(
    choices=SessionRecordingRetentionPeriod.choices,
    default=SessionRecordingRetentionPeriod.THIRTY_DAYS,
)

# After (Django 5.0 compatible, also works on 4.2)
session_recording_retention_period = models.CharField(
    choices=SessionRecordingRetentionPeriod,
    default=SessionRecordingRetentionPeriod.THIRTY_DAYS,
)
```

<details>
<summary>13 files with enum changes</summary>

- `posthog/approvals/models.py`
- `posthog/models/async_deletion/async_deletion.py`
- `posthog/models/data_deletion_request.py`
- `posthog/models/event_ingestion_restriction_config.py`
- `posthog/models/materialized_column_slots.py`
- `posthog/models/team/team.py`
- `posthog/models/user.py`
- `products/data_modeling/backend/models/node.py`
- `products/event_definitions/backend/models/event_definition.py`
- `products/event_definitions/backend/models/property_definition.py`
- `products/event_definitions/backend/models/schema.py`
- `products/llm_analytics/backend/models/provider_keys.py`
- `products/messaging/backend/models/message_category.py`

</details>

#### 2. `HTTP_*` kwargs → `headers={}` (24 test files)

Django 4.2 deprecated the `HTTP_` prefix pattern in test client calls ([docs](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/5.0/releases/4.2/#requests-and-responses)):

```python
# Before (Django 4.2 deprecated)
response = self.client.get(
    "/api/endpoint/",
    HTTP_AUTHORIZATION=f"Bearer {token}",
)

# After (Django 5.0 compatible, also works on 4.2)
response = self.client.get(
    "/api/endpoint/",
    headers={"authorization": f"Bearer {token}"},
)
```

<details>
<summary>24 files with test client changes</summary>

- `ee/api/agentic_provisioning/test/base.py`
- `ee/api/agentic_provisioning/test/test_account_requests.py`
- `ee/api/agentic_provisioning/test/test_authorize.py`
- `ee/api/agentic_provisioning/test/test_e2e_flow.py`
- `ee/api/agentic_provisioning/test/test_health.py`
- `ee/api/agentic_provisioning/test/test_oauth_token.py`
- `ee/api/agentic_provisioning/test/test_region_proxy.py`
- `ee/api/agentic_provisioning/test/test_services.py`
- `ee/api/scim/test/test_scim_request_log.py`
- `ee/api/vercel/test/test_vercel_webhooks.py`
- `posthog/api/test/test_alert.py`
- `posthog/api/test/test_organization_invites.py`
- `posthog/api/test/test_query.py`
- `posthog/api/test/test_remote_config.py`
- `posthog/api/test/test_sharing_access_token_security.py`
- `posthog/api/test/test_toolbar_oauth_endpoint_auth.py`
- `posthog/test/test_internal_api_auth.py`
- `products/conversations/backend/api/tests/test_slack_endpoints.py`
- `products/conversations/backend/api/tests/test_widget.py`
- `products/endpoints/backend/tests/test_endpoint.py`
- `products/mcp_store/backend/test/test_proxy.py`
- `products/slack_app/backend/tests/test_posthog_code_event_handler.py`
- `products/slack_app/backend/tests/test_posthog_code_interactivity.py`
- `products/tasks/backend/tests/test_webhooks.py`

</details>

### Why now

Django 4.2 LTS reaches end-of-life in **April 2026**. These changes are the first step toward upgrading to Django 5.x. The code patterns targeted here are already deprecated in 4.2 and will be removed in future versions.

### Risk

**Zero** — all changes are backward-compatible with Django 4.2. The `choices=EnumClass` form and `headers={}` dict both work on Django 4.2+. No version bump is included in this PR.

## Test plan

- [ ] CI passes (no behavioral changes, only syntax modernization)
- [ ] Spot-check changed files for correctness
- [ ] Verify no model behavior changes (enum choices still work as before)

---

title: feat(llma): add AI-powered taggers for LLM generation classification
body:
## Problem

LLM analytics users need a way to automatically add custom tags to their AI generations (e.g. which product features were discussed, user intent, safety flags). This is conceptually similar to evaluations but produces structured multi-label output instead of boolean pass/fail.

## Changes

Adds a full-stack "Tags" feature to LLM analytics — a standalone product surface at `/llm-analytics/tags` with its own sidebar nav entry, following proven patterns from the evaluations system but fully decoupled.

**Feature flag:** `llm-analytics-tags`

**Backend:**
- `Tagger` Django model with `tagger_type` (LLM or Hog), `tagger_config` (prompt + tag definitions for LLM; source + bytecode for Hog), conditions, model configuration
- CRUD API viewset at `/api/environments/:id/taggers/` with `test_hog` endpoint for testing Hog code against recent generations
- Pydantic validation schemas (`LLMTaggerConfig`, `HogTaggerConfig`), RBAC scope, OpenAPI types generated

**Temporal workflow:**
- `run-tagger` workflow dispatches between LLM and Hog based on `tagger_type`
- LLM path: dynamic Pydantic schema with valid tag names + constraints in field descriptions, structured output, post-validation (strip unknown tags, enforce max)
- Hog path: deterministic bytecode execution, no LLM cost, instant results. Hog code receives `input`, `output`, `properties`, `event`, `tags` globals and returns a list of tag name strings
- `$ai_tag` event emission via `capture_internal`, trial quota (shared with evals)

**Plugin server (Node.js):**
- `TaggerManagerService` — lazy-loaded from Postgres, PubSub reload on config change
- Evaluation scheduler extended to match taggers alongside evals
- `startTaggerRunWorkflow` on `TemporalService`

**Frontend:**
- Standalone scene at `/llm-analytics/tags` with `IconList` sidebar icon
- Tags list page: search, enable/disable toggle, method badge (LLM/Hog), tag pills with description tooltips, prompt tooltip
- Tagger detail page with two tabs: **Runs** (tag events table with timestamp, generation link, tags, reasoning) and **Configuration** (edit form)
- LLM/Hog method selector in form — LLM mode shows prompt + model picker, Hog mode shows code editor with 6 quickstart examples and "Test on recent generations" button with results table
- Model configuration picker (BYOK or PostHog trial), conditions editor (property filters + rollout %)
- 5 default taggers auto-seeded on first visit (topic tags, user intent, safety flags, response complexity, user sentiment) — all disabled, users just toggle on
- Tags tab in trace view for generation events (shows tags applied per tagger, clickable tagger name)
- `TagDisplay` component for `$ai_tag` events in `EventPropertyTabs`
- `TrialUsageMeter` made configurable (shows "Trial runs" on Tags page)

**Taxonomy/schema:**
- `$ai_tag` event type, `llm_tags` icon type in `FileSystemIconType`
- Tagger properties registered in taxonomy

**Tests:**
- Backend model tests (validation, bytecode compilation, signal)
- API tests (CRUD, permissions, search, team isolation)
- Temporal workflow tests (happy path, tag validation, trial limit, emit event, system prompt building)

## How did you test this code?

- End-to-end tested locally: enabled User sentiment tagger, sent demo traces, verified `$ai_tag` events emitted via Temporal workflow, tag results visible in runs tab and trace view
- Hog tagger test endpoint verified against recent generations
- All pre-commit hooks passing

## Publish to changelog?

No — behind feature flag, not yet ready for general availability.

## Docs update

Not yet — will be needed once the feature is ready for beta.

title: feat(ingestion): add event filter evaluation step to ingestion pipeline
body:
## Problem

We need the Node.js ingestion pipeline to evaluate customer-configured event filters and drop (or count in dry run) matching events before they reach transformations.

Stacked on #52649 (migration PR) and `pl/pipelines/batching` (batching framework).

## Changes

Adds event filter evaluation to the Node.js ingestion pipeline:

- **`common/event-filters/`** module:
  - `schema.ts` — zod schemas for filter tree nodes and DB rows, with `EventFilterMode` (disabled/dry_run/live)
  - `evaluate.ts` — recursive tree evaluation with conservative empty-group handling (empty AND = false, not vacuous true), exhaustive operator check
  - `manager.ts` — `EventFilterManager` using `BackgroundRefresher` (60s) to load active filters from Postgres
  - `batch-app-metrics.ts` — `EventFiltersBatchAppMetrics` aggregates metrics per batch instead of producing one Kafka message per matched event, flushed via `queueMessages` at batch end
  - `test-helpers.ts` — shared tree-building helpers (`cond`, `and`, `or`, `not`) mirroring the Python test suite

- **`common/steps/event-filters-steps.ts`** — three pipeline steps:
  - `createEventFiltersBatchAppMetricsBeforeBatchStep` — creates batch metrics in beforeBatch hook, attaches to each element
  - `createApplyEventFiltersStep` — evaluates filters using event headers (runs before message parsing), increments batch metrics
  - `createFlushEventFiltersBatchAppMetricsStep` — afterBatch step that flushes aggregated metrics as a side effect

- **Wiring**: `EventFilterManager` created in `ingestion-consumer.ts`, batch metrics flow through `joined-ingestion-pipeline` (beforeBatch → batch context → afterBatch)

## How did you test this code?

95 automated tests across 4 test suites:

- **`event-filters-steps.test.ts`** (43 tests): step behavior (no filter, live drop, dry_run pass-through, metrics increment) plus all filter evaluation scenarios via `it.each` — condition matching, empty groups, boolean logic, complex trees including the Python `test_complex_tree_with_many_test_cases`
- **`batch-app-metrics.test.ts`** (7 tests): aggregation, flush via `queueMessages`, key separation, clearing after flush
- **`schema.test.ts`** (18 tests): zod validation for conditions, groups, nodes, rows — mirrors Python `TestValidateFilterTree`/`TestValidateCondition`/`TestValidateNodeStructure`
- **`manager.integration.test.ts`** (36 tests): end-to-end with real Postgres — loading, disabled/null/empty filters, multi-team, `treeHasConditions` guard, complex evaluation, plus 14 resilience tests for corrupt data (wrong types, unknown fields/operators, partial trees)

Tested on the PoC branch with live ingestion.

## Publish to changelog?

No

title: docs: clarify LLM Gateway rate limiting behavior for personal API keys
body:
## Summary

Updates the LLM Gateway README to document the rate limiting behavior change from PR #52638.

## Changes

- **User attribution section**: Clarified that OAuth tokens use the token holder's user ID, while personal API keys use the end-user identifier from the request body (`user` for OpenAI, `metadata.user_id` for Anthropic) for both analytics and rate limiting
- **Code example comments**: Updated to indicate the `user` / `metadata.user_id` fields are used for "End-user attribution and rate limiting" (not just attribution)
- **User-level limits section**: Added explicit documentation explaining how `end_user_id` is resolved differently for OAuth tokens vs personal API keys

## Related PR

Closes documentation gap from #52638

title: feat(llma): add summary and cluster event counts to usage report

labels.name: team/llm-analytics
labels.description: LLM Analytics

body:
## Problem

The LLM analytics usage report does not track summarization and clustering events, so we have no visibility into adoption of these features across organizations.

## Changes

Add four new metrics to the LLM analytics usage report:
- `ai_trace_summary_count` - count of `$ai_trace_summary` events
- `ai_generation_summary_count` - count of `$ai_generation_summary` events
- `ai_trace_clusters_count` - count of `$ai_trace_clusters` events
- `ai_generation_clusters_count` - count of `$ai_generation_clusters` events

These events are already in the `AIEventType` enum and included in the `AI_EVENTS` list, so they were already being scanned in the query but not counted individually.

## How did you test this code?

- Ran `test_get_all_ai_metrics` - verifies the new counts are correctly extracted from the combined query
- Ran `test_full_llm_analytics_report` - verifies end-to-end report generation with the new metrics aggregated by org
- Ran `test_prompt_fetched_only_team_generates_report_with_zero_ai_metrics` - verifies zero counts when no summary/cluster events exist

title: fix: use codespace-aware URLs for Vite dev assets
body:
## Problem

PostHog renders a blank page when accessed via GitHub Codespaces. The Django backend correctly sets `window.JS_URL` to the Codespace-forwarded origin, but the Vite dev script tags (`@react-refresh`, `@vite/client`, `src/index.tsx`) are hardcoded to `http://localhost:8234`, which is unreachable from the browser in Codespaces where forwarded ports use the pattern `https://<CODESPACE_NAME>-<PORT>.<DOMAIN>`.

## Changes

Four independent `http://localhost:8234` references replaced with Codespace-aware equivalents:

- **`posthog/settings/__init__.py`** -- Auto-detect Codespace via `CODESPACE_NAME` and
`GITHUB_CODESPACES_PORT_FORWARDING_DOMAIN` env vars (set automatically by GitHub) to construct the correct default `JS_URL`

- **`posthog/utils.py`** -- `vite_dev_scripts` now uses `get_js_url(request)` instead of hardcoding
localhost

- **`posthog/middleware.py`** -- CSP `resource_url` and `connect_debug_url` derive from `settings.JS_URL`, using `wss://` for HTTPS origins

- **`frontend/vite.config.ts`** -- `server.origin` and `server.host` are Codespace-aware for correct HMR asset URL generation

Local dev behavior is unchanged -- all defaults remain `http://localhost:8234` when the Codespace env vars are absent.

## How did you test this code?

- Verified `JS_URL` resolves correctly in both environments:
  - Local: `http://localhost:8234`
  - Simulated Codespace (`CODESPACE_NAME=test GITHUB_CODESPACES_PORT_FORWARDING_DOMAIN=app.github.dev`): `https://test-8234.app.github.dev`
- CSP middleware tests pass (`TestCSPMiddleware`)
- Linting clean (`ruff check`)

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

N/A

title: chore: generalize SQL sources into a single class
body: ## Problem Warehouse SQL sources each reimplemented the same orchestration: open an SSH tunnel, pull schemas (and optionally FKs and row counts), map connection failures to user-facing errors, and build the pipeline source. Shared column/table types lived in a flat `common/sql.py` without a base class, so there was no single place to evolve that flow—unlike `rest_source`, which already centralizes REST-shaped sources. ## Changes Introduce **`posthog/temporal/data_imports/sources/common/sql_source/`** as shared SQL warehouse plumbing (same general shape as `rest_source/`): - **`base.SQLSource`** — central `get_schemas`, `validate_credentials`, and `source_for_pipeline` built from class-level collaborators (`_schema_fetcher`, `_incremental_filter`, `_source_creator`) and optional hooks for DB-specific behavior inside the SSH tunnel and during credential checks. - **`typing.py`** — `Column`, `Table`, `TableBase`, `TableReference`, `TableSchemas`, etc., moved out of `common/sql.py`, plus aliases the base uses (`ForeignKeyMapping`, `RowCountMapping`, `ExceptionHandler`, `ConnectionErrorMap`, `SchemaColumns`, …). - **`exceptions.py`** — `SQLSourceError` and `SSLRequiredError` (moved from `postgres/postgres.py`). - **`common/sql.py`** — re-exports typing symbols only so existing `from …common.sql import …` call sites stay valid without edits. - **Tests** — `common/test/test_sql.py` renamed to `sql_source/tests/test_typing.py`; new **`sql_source/tests/test_base.py`** covers tunnel wiring, FK/row-count hooks, `names` filtering, credential validation order, connection-error substring mapping, SSH tunnel errors, and extra exception handlers. **Hooks / extension points on `SQLSource`** (subclasses override only what they need): - `_get_foreign_keys` / `_get_row_counts` — enrich `SourceSchema` (row counts only when `with_counts=True`). - `_get_connection_error_class` / `_get_connection_error_map` — map connection exceptions to user-facing strings. - `_get_extra_exception_handlers` — run before generic handling (e.g. SSL-required). - `_get_extra_source_kwargs` — extra kwargs passed into `_source_creator` for pipeline runs. **`PostgresSource`** now subclasses `SQLSource`: the previous hand-rolled `get_schemas`, `validate_credentials`, and `source_for_pipeline` are removed in favor of the static/class wiring above plus Postgres hook implementations. `PostgresErrors` is renamed to `_POSTGRES_ERROR_MAP`; the display name for errors uses `source_display_name` from the base. **`PostgreSQLColumn.to_arrow_field`** in `postgres/postgres.py` — table-driven **`_POSTGRES_TYPE_MAP`** with small special cases for numeric precision/scale and arrays instead of a long per-type `match`. **Scope:** only **Postgres** is migrated onto `SQLSource` in this PR; other SQL sources are unchanged aside from continuing to use `common.sql` types through the shim. ## How did you test this code? - New `sql_source` orchestration tests + existing Postgres / typing coverage; `ruff check` + `ruff format` clean - Tested locally with local Postgres as source ## Publish to changelog? No

title: feat: add d3 + canvas LineChart
body: ## Problem There's too many different charts, any time we make a fix, add a new feature, we need to do it in a bunch of different places and it doesn't really seem to happen. 💡 We need a single set of charts, that every product re-uses. ## Changes In this PR we just add some core stuff: a canvas, d3 setting up the basics. No actual change, we're using none of this yet. I've done quite a lot more than this though, which is on a [d3 branch here](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/compare/sam/d3). I've tried to strip it back so it's somewhat reviewable 😅 This is where it's at: So a simple LineChart working, looking a bit like our existing one. There's still a lot of work to do though! Just want to confirm the direction I take this first 😄 ## Why d3 and canvas My thinking is: - We really want 1 charting library, this helps with doing things once and making it all consistent. We can't only have Chart.js or similar, we've already found it's not capable of all the visualisations we need and as we grow we're bound to need more. - A lot of what we do is presenting data, d3 lets us fully customise it and make some amazing visualisations, we're not going to be limited by what plugins we can find. - The advantages of using something like chart.js as doing all this yourself is hard, but this really wasn't with todays tools. - Canvas - We need this to perform well at thousands of data points, we can't get that rendering DOM nodes ## How it works At it's simplest this can just be: ```tsx ``` and we'll expose different `BarChart`, `PieChart`... etc, later too. You can then customise it: ```tsx ``` - Config: allows editing display options, axis format etc - CustomTooltip: This can override a basic default tooltip, but is just content, no redoing positioning code! 😅 - `` is an example of how certain products might need to customize their charts. For example in product-analytics we'll need an `` overlay. The wrapping Chart component will expose all the things you could need via context so in Annotations, you can do something like: ```tsx const Annotations = () => ( const { labels, x, y, scale } = useChart() return {labels.map(...)} ) ``` this should stop any mixing of product specific code, each team can own their own overlays separately. Plus it forces splitting custom chart code into components which I think is a good thing. Hopefully no more giant chart component files! ### Structure Split into some different layers, I think should make this nice and easy to work with. | Layer | Directory | What it does | |---|---|---| | **Charts** | `charts/` | Chart specific stuff, e.g. line/bar/pie. Each chart type provides its own `createScales` and `draw` to the base. | | **Core** | `core/` | A shared core Chart component that does canvas setup, draw, interactions, context, error boundary. Chart types above will all render this. | | **Canvas rendering** | `core/canvas-renderer.ts`, `core/scales.ts` | Pure functions that draw to canvas and set up d3 scales. No React, no state. | | **Overlays** | `overlays/` | React components layered on top of the canvas: axes, crosshair, goal lines etc. Use `useChart()` for scales/dimensions. | ## How did you test this code? Temporarily rendering it in our Trends component. I've also got a bunch of tests on another branch, but it's just way too big ## Publish to changelog? No

title: feat(error-tracking): exception steps and adaptive loading
body:
## Problem

The SessionTimeline component used a hardcoded batch size of 25 and a fixed 6-hour query time range. This caused:
- Over-fetching on small containers and under-fetching on large ones
- Missed data when events were more than 6 hours apart
- One-at-a-time merge loop across all loaders (~5-10 queries per direction)
- Console log entries duplicated due to `uuid()` ID generation
- Potential infinite request loops when API returned the same items repeatedly
- Exception steps category shown even when `$exception_steps` property was absent

## Changes

### Smart loading (`SessionTimeline.tsx`)
- Dynamic batch size calculated from container height (`clientHeight / 32px × 1.5`)
- Imperative auto-fill loop replaces fragile `useEffect` — loads until container overflows or data exhausted
- Empty state ("No items") when no data is available

### Adaptive time ranges (`items/base.tsx`)
- Configurable `TimeRangeConfig` replaces hardcoded 6h window
- Default `[1, 6, 24]` — starts narrow (1h) for ClickHouse partition pruning, expands adaptively
- `MAX_FETCH_ITERATIONS = 5` hard cap prevents runaway loops
- Cache-growth guard breaks when no new items are added

### Batch loading (`item-collector.ts`)
- Parallel `previousBatch`/`nextBatch` across all loaders → merge-sort → pick top N
- Reduces queries from ~5-10 per direction to exactly **1 per loader per direction**

### Bug fixes
- **Deterministic log IDs** (`items/logs.tsx`): `timestamp-level-message` instead of `uuid()` — prevents duplicate entries
- **Exception steps visibility** (`SessionTab/index.tsx`): category only added when `$exception_steps` is an array
- **Malformed step reasons** (`items/exceptionSteps.tsx`): per-step validation errors shown (e.g., "step 1: missing name, offset_ms")
- **Scroll observer** (`useScrollObserver.tsx`): threshold-based (100px) replaces ratio-based detection

### Storybook
- 8 new `SessionTimeline` stories (Default, Short, Tall, FewItems, Empty, SlowLoading, ManyItems, SingleCategory)
- New `ExceptionCardSessionTimelineWithoutSteps` story
- Enriched mock data for existing ExceptionCard timeline stories

## How did you test this code?

- Smart fill adapts to container height (Short=20, Default=48, Tall=76 items)
- Empty state shows "No items", no infinite loops
- ExceptionCard stories: 12 unique items with steps, 9 without steps, 11 with malformed steps
- 0 duplicate item IDs across all stories
- Request count: 18 per ExceptionCard story (down from infinite/45+)

## Publish to changelog?

no

N/A

title: feat(phai): Add pagination and well-known properties to taxonomy queries

labels.name: update-snapshots
labels.description: Enable auto commit snapshots for Storybook and Playwright

body:
## Problem

The event and team taxonomy query runners need to support pagination and include well-known properties/events in their responses. Currently, they return all results without pagination support and don't include well-known properties/events that may not have been discovered in the data.

## Changes

### Core Functionality
- **Pagination Support**: Added `limit` and `offset` parameters to `EventTaxonomyQuery` and `TeamTaxonomyQuery` with a default limit of 500
- **Well-Known Properties/Events**: Integrated `WELL_KNOWN_EVENT_PROPERTY_NAMES` and `WELL_KNOWN_EVENT_NAMES` from the taxonomy module
- **Smart Appending**: Well-known properties/events are only appended to results when:
  - On the last page (when `hasMore=False`)
  - Not querying for specific properties (discovery mode only)
  - Not already present in the results (no duplicates)
- **Response Fields**: Added `hasMore`, `limit`, and `offset` fields to both `EventTaxonomyQueryResponse` and `CachedEventTaxonomyQueryResponse`

### Implementation Details
- Updated `EventTaxonomyQueryRunner` to use `HogQLHasMorePaginator` for handling pagination
- Updated `TeamTaxonomyQueryRunner` to append well-known events on the last page
- Modified HogQL queries to use `LIMIT offset+1` to detect if more results exist
- Added comprehensive test coverage for pagination, well-known items, and edge cases

### Schema Updates
- Updated `schema.py` and `schema.json` to include new pagination fields
- Updated TypeScript schema definitions in `schema-general.ts`

## How did you test this code?

Added extensive test coverage including:
- `test_well_known_properties_appended_in_discovery_mode`: Verifies well-known properties are appended in discovery mode
- `test_well_known_properties_not_appended_with_specific_properties`: Ensures well-known properties aren't appended when querying specific properties
- `test_well_known_properties_not_duplicated`: Confirms no duplicate entries when well-known properties appear in data
- `test_pagination_with_limit_and_offset`: Tests pagination with limit and offset parameters
- `test_well_known_properties_only_on_last_page`: Verifies well-known properties only appear on the last page
- Similar tests for team taxonomy queries

All existing tests were updated to account for the new well-known items in results, filtering by `sample_count > 0` or `count > 0` to isolate actual data results from well-known items.

## Publish to changelog?

Yes - this adds pagination support and well-known properties/events discovery to the taxonomy query APIs.

title: feat(phai): dynamic scan period for AI taxonomy based on org event volume
body:
## Problem

PostHog AI naively retrieves data schema from ClickHouse for all orgs regardless of their size. This PR changes the way we retrieve events and properties:
- We'll retrieve events from Postgres for smaller orgs with low volume and event cardinality (<50).
- We'll retrieve from ClickHouse if there are more than 50 events with an increased/reduced scan window.

The main goal is to speed up and improve the accuracy of event/property retrieval across various org sizes.

Gated with a feature flag.

## Changes

### New Module: `posthog/hogql_queries/ai/scan_period.py`
- Added `TaxonomyVolumeTier` enum with four tiers: LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, EXTRA_HIGH
- Implemented `get_taxonomy_volume_tier()` to classify orgs based on billing usage data
- Implemented `get_scan_period_days()` to return dynamic scan periods:
  - LOW: 7 days
  - MEDIUM: 30 days
  - HIGH: 7 days
  - EXTRA_HIGH: 3 days
- Implemented `should_use_postgres_for_events()` to determine data source (Postgres for LOW tier, ClickHouse otherwise)

### Updated Query Runners
- `EventTaxonomyQueryRunner`: Now uses dynamic scan period via `get_scan_period_days()`
- `TeamTaxonomyQueryRunner`: Now uses dynamic scan period via `get_scan_period_days()`

### Updated Event Processing
- `ee/hogai/utils/helpers.py`:
  - Split `_process_events_data()` into two fetch methods:
    - `_fetch_events_from_postgres()`: Queries EventDefinition for low-volume orgs, ordered by `last_seen_at`
    - `_fetch_events_from_clickhouse()`: Uses TeamTaxonomyQueryRunner for higher-volume orgs
  - Updated `_process_events_data()` to route to appropriate data source based on volume tier
  - Updated `format_events_yaml()` to accept optional `volume_tier` parameter

### Updated Tools
- `ee/hogai/tools/read_taxonomy/core.py`: Passes `volume_tier` to event formatting functions

## How did you test this code?

Added comprehensive test suite in `posthog/hogql_queries/ai/test/test_scan_period.py`:
- **TestTaxonomyVolumeTier**: 13 tests covering all volume tier boundaries and edge cases (missing data, None values, boundary conditions)
- **TestGetScanPeriodDays**: 4 tests verifying correct scan period for each tier
- **TestShouldUsePostgresForEvents**: 4 tests verifying correct data source selection

All tests pass and cover the full range of volume thresholds and edge cases.

## Publish to changelog?

No

title: docs: Document hogli doctor:zombies command for cleaning up orphaned processes
body:
## Summary

Documents the new `hogli doctor:zombies` command added in #52515 in the developing-locally handbook guide.

## Changes

Added a new "Orphaned dev processes" entry to the "Common gotchas" section in `docs/published/handbook/engineering/developing-locally.md`:

> **Orphaned dev processes**
> If phrocs or mprocs exits uncleanly (crash, force quit, etc.), child processes can survive as orphans, consuming memory and CPU. These orphans can also cause port conflicts on the next start. Run `hogli doctor:zombies` to find and kill them. Use `--dry-run` to preview what would be killed without actually killing anything.

This entry is placed after the "Port conflicts" entry since orphaned processes are a common cause of port binding errors.

title: feat(replay): surface asset errors and rrweb warnings in doctor tab

labels.name: update-snapshots
labels.description: Enable auto commit snapshots for Storybook and Playwright

body:
## Problem

When viewing a session replay, hundreds of debug messages (CSP violations, asset load failures, rrweb warnings) are dumped to the browser console where they're buried in noise and hard to find. Support engineers and developers debugging replay issues have to sift through walls of repeated `caughtAssetErrorFromIframe` logs and `[PostHog Replayer] N warnings` messages.

## Changes

Surface this information in the inspector's Doctor tab instead, grouped and humanized:

- **Asset errors** are grouped by type (CSP violations sub-grouped by directive, other errors by resource type with deduplicated URLs)
- **RRWeb warnings** are categorized by message and shown with counts
- **"Could not find node with id" warnings** are filtered out as non-actionable noise
- Console spam (`console.log('caughtAssetErrorFromIframe', ...)` and `[PostHog Replayer] N warnings`) is removed

Performance-conscious design:
- Asset errors are grouped on write (O(1) per error via mutable cache structure), not accumulated as raw arrays
- Both asset errors and rrweb warnings are stored in kea `cache`, not reducers
- A single 2s debounced flush timer pushes one combined `flushDoctorDiagnostics` action to one reducer
- Zero kea dispatches per individual error

New files:
- `asset-error-grouping.ts` — pure types and functions for incremental grouping
- `player-logging.test.ts` — parameterized tests for warning categorization
- `groupAssetErrors.test.ts` — parameterized tests for asset error grouping
- `ItemDoctor.stories.tsx` — storybook stories for all doctor item variants

## How did you test this code?

- 31 parameterized unit tests covering `categorizeWarning`, `isIgnoredWarning`, `addAssetError`, `formatGroupedAssetErrors`, and inspector list filtering
- Storybook stories for visual verification of all doctor item types (CSP violations, asset errors, rrweb warnings, snapshot events, config)
- I am an agent and have not tested this manually beyond code-based tests

## Publish to changelog?

no

## 🤖 LLM context

Co-authored with Claude Opus 4.6. The design went through two review iterations (paul-reviewer and xp-reviewer) which shaped the final architecture: group-on-write instead of group-on-read, cache-first to minimize kea work, and extracting the grouping logic into its own file.

title: Perform all development inside docker containers
body:
This is a draft PR to showcase how we might sandbox local development processes in a docker container. Source code is mounted from the local host.

## Problem                                                                                                                                                                                                        

Local dev processes (Django, Vite, Node, Rust, Celery) run directly on the host with full access to the host filesystem and environment variables. There's no sandboxing — a misbehaving process or dependency can read `~/.ssh`, `~/.aws`, host env vars, etc.

## Changes                             

Adds an optional sandboxed dev mode that runs all app processes inside a Docker container while keeping infra services (Postgres, Redis, ClickHouse, Kafka) in the existing docker-compose setup.                 

**New files:**                                                                                                                                                                                                    
- `Dockerfile.dev` — Dev image with Python 3.12, Node 24, Rust toolchain, uv, sqlx-cli, sccache, and phrocs. No source code baked in — repo is bind-mounted for hot-reload.
- `docker-compose.dev-sandboxed.yml` — Compose config for the dev container. Joins `posthog_default` network with DNS aliases (`web`, `capture`, `plugins`) so Caddy routes traffic correctly. Explicit env var allowlist — no host env leakage.                                                                                                                                                                                  
- `docker-compose.dev-sandboxed-proxy.yml` — Proxy override that removes `extra_hosts` entries so Caddy resolves service names via Docker DNS to the dev container.                                               
- `bin/start-sandboxed` — Entry point: starts infra, generates a patched mprocs config (strips `docker-compose` process, swaps `wait-for-docker` → `wait-for-infra`), launches the dev container with phrocs.     
- `bin/wait-for-infra` — Docker-socket-free alternative to `bin/wait-for-docker`. Checks infra readiness via TCP instead of `docker inspect`.                                                                     

**Modified files (backward-compatible):**                                                                                                                                                                         
- `bin/start-backend` — `OBJECT_STORAGE_ENDPOINT` and `SESSION_RECORDING_V2_S3_ENDPOINT` now respect existing env vars via `${VAR:-default}`. Added `POSTHOG_SANDBOXED` check to bind to `0.0.0.0` inside          container.                                                                                                                                                                                                        
- `bin/start-rust-service` — All hardcoded `localhost` URLs converted to `${VAR:-localhost:...}` so compose env vars take precedence inside the container. No behavioral change when running on host (defaults are identical).                                                                                                                                                                                                      

**Security isolation:**                                                                                                                                                                                           
- No host env vars leak (explicit allowlist)
- Processes cannot access `~/.ssh`, `~/.aws`, `~/.config`, etc.                                                                                                                                                   
- No Docker socket mounted                                                                                                                                                                                        
- Non-root user inside container (entrypoint drops to `posthog` via gosu after fixing volume ownership)                                                                                                           
- Named volumes for `node_modules`/`.venv`/cargo caches prevent host writes outside repo                                                                                                                          

## How did you test this code?                                                                                                                                                                                    

Ran `bin/start-sandboxed` end-to-end:                                                                                                                                                                             
- All phrocs processes start successfully (backend, frontend, celery, ingestion, capture, feature-flags, migrations)
- Django responds on `localhost:8000`                                                                                                                                                                             
- Vite HMR works — editing frontend files triggers hot reload
- Backend auto-reloads on Python file changes                                                                                                                                                                     
- Rust services compile and connect to infra via Docker DNS                                                                                                                                                       
- `docker exec` confirms only allowlisted env vars are present                                                                                                                                                    
- Non-sandboxed `bin/start` still works with no behavioral change

title: fix(insights): position annotation badges at data points
body:
## Problem

Annotation badges on trend charts are positioned at Chart.js visible tick positions, which can be very sparse on wide date ranges. On a 90-day weekly chart with only 3 visible tick labels ("2026", "February", "March"), the last tick covers 4+ weeks of data. An annotation for March 23 appears at the March 1 tick position, roughly 3 weeks before its actual date. The popover also misleadingly says "Week of March 1, 2026" instead of the correct week.

This happens because `AnnotationsOverlay` reads `chart.scales.x.ticks` (the sparse autoSkip'd ticks) and positions badges only at those points. The `determineAnnotationsDateGroup` modular arithmetic correctly groups the annotation into the last tick's bucket, but the badge renders at that tick's x-position, far from the annotation's actual date.

**A couple of things I want to flag on this PR:

1. I have some concerns about performance on very dense charts. This will create a lot more badge elements than were created previously.
2.  The badge width CSS uses the data point interval. For 14 weekly points on a typical chart, that's ~43px per badge, which is fine. For 365 daily points it'd be ~1.6px, making the "hover to add annotation" interaction essentially unusable. I would argue it wasn't great before, so not sure about the best approach here.**

## Changes

- **`useAnnotationsPositioning.ts`**: Compute pixel interval from all chart data points (`chart._metasets[0].data`) instead of just the visible Chart.js ticks, so the interval matches data point spacing.
- **`AnnotationsOverlay.tsx`**: Generate ticks from all data point indices instead of reading sparse ticks from `chart.scales.x.ticks`. This makes `pointsPerTick = 1`, so every data point gets its own badge and annotations map directly to their `startOf(interval)` date without modular offset.

No changes to `annotationsOverlayLogic.ts` - its grouping logic works correctly when `pointsPerTick = 1`.

## How did you test this code?

I've not manually tested yet, but here's what I will try:
1. Create a trends insight with weekly interval and "Last 90 days" date range
3. Create a project-level annotation for a date in the most recent week (e.g., today)
4. **Before fix**: The annotation badge snaps to the nearest visible tick label, potentially weeks before the annotation date. The popover title shows the tick's date, not the annotation's week.
5. **After fix**: The annotation badge appears at the correct data point position matching the annotation's week. The popover title shows the correct week.

## Publish to changelog?

no

## Docs update

no

## 🤖 LLM context

Co-authored by Claude Code (Opus).

title: fix(taxonomic-filter): stabilize eventNames selector
body:
<!-- mendral:session:action:01KMNHGK8DQ3A7KP4R47RAMVPC -->
## Summary

- Fixes the `PropertyFilters › add filter: click add, search, select property, verify onChange shape` Jest test failure caused by an unstable reselect input.
- The `eventNames` selector was returning a new `[]` array reference on every call when `props.eventNames` is undefined, causing `taxonomicGroupTypes` → `infiniteListLogics` to recompute and reset on every render, losing search results before they could appear.
- Fix: replace the inline `[]` fallback with a module-level `EMPTY_ARRAY` constant so the same reference is returned every time, preventing unnecessary downstream recomputations.

Resolves insight: Jest: PropertyFilters search results don't appear after taxonomicGroupTypes now depends on unstable eventNames

body:
## Changes

Fix local Playwright config to match how the dev stack actually works:
- Default baseURL to localhost:8010 (Caddy proxy) instead of 8080
- Set local retries to 0 (no point retrying during dev)
- Set workers to 3 (stack can't handle more reliably)

Add bin/e2e-db-snapshot for fast DB resets via pg_dump/pg_restore instead of re-running all 1200+ migrations every time. First run creates a snapshot, subsequent resets restore from it in seconds.

title: feat(experiments): add copy experiment to another project
body: ## Problem There's no way to copy an experiment from one project to another within the same organization. Users with multi-project setups have to manually recreate experiments, which is tedious and error-prone. ## Changes - Add `copy_to_project` API endpoint on the experiments viewset that duplicates an experiment (with its metrics, parameters, and feature flag) into a target project as a new draft - The feature flag is created disabled in the target project, or reuses an existing flag with the same key - Saved metrics and holdouts are intentionally skipped since they are team-scoped - Legacy metric formats are rejected with a clear error - Authorization checks ensure the target project is in the same org and the user has access - Add a "Copy to another project" section in the duplicate experiment modal with a project selector (only shown when the org has multiple projects) ## Testing https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8abd4b86-8746-4525-8205-f613f5c99a00 - Added tests for the happy path (copy with metrics, parameters, stats config, exposure criteria) - Added tests for feature flag behavior (creates disabled flag, reuses existing flag) - Added tests for saved metrics and holdouts being skipped - Added tests for authorization (cross-org blocked, missing target)

title: feat(models): add fail-closed TeamScopedManager for tenant isolation

labels.name: waiting
labels.description: Prevents stale-bot from marking the PR as stale.

body:
**Context**
PostHog has ~263 Django models, ~35 of which get team scope only indirectly through FK chains. There's no automated enforcement that queries are team-scoped — it's code review only.

Continues haacked's work from #47073 (`haacked/auto-scope-team-id`, Feb 2026) and makes the key architectural change: **fail-closed by default**. Querying a scoped model without team context raises `TeamScopeError` instead of silently returning all rows.

**What's in here**
- `posthog/models/scoping/` module: ContextVar-based team context, `team_scope()` context manager, `@with_team_scope()` Celery decorator
- `TeamScopedManager` — raises `TeamScopeError` when no context is set. Escape hatches: `.unscoped()`, `.for_team(id)`
- `TeamScopingMiddleware` — sets team context from `request.user.current_team_id` on every request
- `ProductTeamModel` — abstract base for multi-DB products (plain `BigIntegerField` team_id, no FK)
- Semgrep rule catching unscoped model access in Celery tasks
- Visual review wired up as first consumer: all 4 models on `ProductTeamModel`, Celery task takes `team_id`, management command wrapped in `team_scope()`, tests use autouse fixture

**Limitations (documented in README)**
This is defense-in-depth, not a complete security boundary. Django's `_base_manager` bypasses custom managers for related-object access, and raw SQL bypasses the ORM entirely. FK traversal (`run.snapshots.all()`) is safe because the FK constrains results, but no team_id filter is applied. Full enforcement would need Postgres RLS.

**Test plan**
- [ ] Scoping module unit tests pass (`test_scoping.py`, `test_manager.py`, `test_middleware.py`, `test_product_mixin.py`)
- [ ] Visual review tests pass with autouse `team_scope` fixture
- [ ] Celery task signature change verified in task tests

title: feat: bound number of concurrent buffered files during s3 upload

labels.name: smoke_test:kafka-deduplicator
labels.description: Build kafka-deduplicator smoke test image

body:
## Problem

During checkpoint uploads, RAM usage spikes above 10GB. The root cause is that `FuturesUnordered` eagerly polls all upload futures simultaneously, allocating ~18MB per file (8MB read buffer + ~10MB `BufWriter`). With multiple partitions checkpointing concurrently, hundreds of upload buffers are alive at once even though the `LimitStore` semaphore only limits S3 API calls, not buffer allocations.

## Changes

- Replace `FuturesUnordered` with `stream::iter(...).buffer_unordered(N)` in the S3 uploader so only N upload futures are polled (and buffered) at a time per partition
- Add a new `MAX_UPLOAD_BUFFERS_PER_PARTITION` config (default 25) that controls per-partition upload concurrency, separate from the global `MAX_CONCURRENT_CHECKPOINT_FILE_UPLOADS` which continues to control the `LimitStore` S3 API semaphore
- Change the default Kafka compression codec from snappy to lz4
